description: A complete online storefront with product listings, cart functionality, and checkout flow.
title: E-Commerce Store

sections.Project Overview: This e-commerce store project simulates a real production environment where you build a complete online storefront from scratch using React. You will implement a product catalog with categories and filters, a shopping cart with full CRUD operations, a multi-step checkout process, and Stripe payment integration. The project covers state management with React Context and useReducer, API integration with a mock backend (or Node.js/Express server), responsive product grids, image galleries, and order management. The architecture follows industry patterns used by companies like Shopify, BigCommerce, and Magento.
sections.Problem Statement: Building an e-commerce site involves complex state management (cart, user, products), secure payment processing, responsive product displays, and a smooth checkout flow. Many developers rely on platforms like Shopify or WooCommerce but don't understand the underlying architecture. The challenge is building a custom e-commerce solution that handles: concurrent cart updates, inventory management, tax calculations, shipping logic, payment gateway integration, order confirmation, and error recovery - all while providing a seamless user experience.
sections.Real World Use Cases: Small Business Storefront: A local boutique sells handmade products online with categories, size/color variants, and local pickup options. Digital Products Marketplace: A creator sells digital downloads (ebooks, templates, software) with instant delivery after payment and license key generation. Multi-vendor Platform: A marketplace connects multiple vendors with customers, each vendor managing their own inventory, pricing, and shipping profiles.
sections.Industry Relevance: Global e-commerce sales exceeded $5.7 trillion in 2025 and continue growing. Companies like Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce power millions of stores. Understanding e-commerce architecture is valuable for roles at any tech company that sells products online. React is used by 40%+ of professional developers and is the foundation for modern e-commerce frontends (Shopify Hydrogen, Next.js Commerce). Stripe processes payments for millions of businesses worldwide and has the most developer-friendly API in the payments industry.
sections.Learning Outcomes: Architect a React application with component hierarchy, reusable UI components, and container/presentational patterns Implement complex state management using React Context API with useReducer for cart, auth, and checkout state Integrate Stripe Elements for secure payment collection with client-side card validation and 3D Secure authentication Build a multi-step checkout flow with form validation, shipping calculation, and order review Implement product filtering with multiple criteria (category, price range, rating, attributes) using URL query parameters Handle cart operations with optimistic updates, quantity management, and persistent cart via localStorage Build a RESTful API backend with Node.js/Express for product management, order processing, and Stripe webhooks Implement responsive product grids with image lazy loading, zoom functionality, and variant selection
sections.Technologies Used: React 18 Context API + useReducer Stripe Elements Node.js/Express MongoDB / PostgreSQL Shipping API Font Awesome CSS Modules
sections.Prerequisites: Solid React fundamentals: components, props, state, useEffect, custom hooks Experience with React Router for client-side navigation and route parameters Understanding of REST API concepts and HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) Node.js and npm/yarn installed locally Basic understanding of asynchronous JavaScript and async/await Stripe account (free) for API keys - sign up at stripe.com
sections.Architecture Overview: The application follows a microservices-inspired architecture with a React frontend and a Node.js/Express backend. The frontend uses React Router for client-side routing with pages for Home, Products, Product Detail, Cart, Checkout, and Order Confirmation. State management is split into contexts: CartContext (items, totals, quantity), AuthContext (user, token, login/logout), and UIContext (modals, notifications, loading states). The backend exposes RESTful endpoints: /api/products (CRUD with filtering), /api/cart (sync), /api/orders (create, history), /api/checkout (payment intent), and /api/webhooks/stripe (payment confirmation). Stripe Elements securely collects payment details without sensitive data touching the server. Product images are served from a CDN (Cloudinary or similar) with responsive srcset for different screen sizes.
sections.Folder Structure: ecommerce-store/ ├── client/ # React frontend │ ├── public/ │ ├── src/ │ │ ├── components/ # Reusable UI components │ │ │ ├── Layout/ │ │ │ ├── Product/ │ │ │ ├── Cart/ │ │ │ └── Checkout/ │ │ ├── contexts/ # State management │ │ │ ├── CartContext.js │ │ │ ├── AuthContext.js │ │ │ └── UIContext.js │ │ ├── hooks/ # Custom hooks │ │ ├── pages/ # Route pages │ │ ├── services/ # API calls │ │ ├── utils/ # Helpers │ │ └── App.js │ └── package.json ├── server/ # Node.js backend │ ├── controllers/ │ ├── models/ │ ├── routes/ │ ├── middleware/ │ ├── config/ │ └── server.js ├── .env.example ├── docker-compose.yml ├── .gitignore └── README.md
sections.Development Roadmap: Phase 1: Project Setup & Component Architecture Initialize React app with Create React App or Vite. Set up React Router with routes for all pages. Build the layout components (Navbar, Footer, Sidebar). Create the component hierarchy and establish the CSS module naming convention. Phase 2: State Management with Context + useReducer Implement CartContext with actions: ADD_ITEM, REMOVE_ITEM, UPDATE_QUANTITY, CLEAR_CART. Implement AuthContext with login/logout/register. Implement UIContext for modals, toasts, and loading states. Add localStorage persistence for cart and auth token. Phase 3: Product Catalog & Filtering Build the product listing page with a responsive grid. Implement filtering by category, price range, rating, and attributes. Add sorting (price low/high, newest, rating). Implement the product detail page with image gallery, variant selection, and add-to-cart. Phase 4: Cart & Checkout Flow Build the cart page with quantity controls, remove button, and price calculation. Implement the multi-step checkout: shipping info, payment method, order review. Add form validation with error messages. Implement the order confirmation page. Phase 5: Stripe Payment Integration Integrate Stripe Elements for card payment collection. Create a PaymentIntent on the server. Handle 3D Secure authentication. Implement Stripe webhooks for payment confirmation and order status updates. Test with Stripe test cards.
sections.Implementation Guide: Step 1: Cart Context with useReducer Implement a robust cart state management system using React Context and useReducer. The reducer handles all cart operations with immutability and supports both client-side and server-synced cart states. const CartContext = createContext(); const initialState = { items: [], shippingAddress: null, paymentMethod: null, couponCode: null, discount: 0, loading: false, error: null }; function cartReducer(state, action) { switch (action.type) { case 'ADD_ITEM': { const existingIndex = state.items.findIndex( i => i.id === action.payload.id && i.variant === action.payload.variant ); if (existingIndex >= 0) { const updatedItems = [...state.items]; updatedItems[existingIndex] = { ...updatedItems[existingIndex], quantity: updatedItems[existingIndex].quantity + action.payload.quantity }; return { ...state, items: updatedItems }; } return { ...state, items: [...state.items, { ...action.payload }] }; } case 'REMOVE_ITEM': return { ...state, items: state.items.filter(i => i.id !== action.payload) }; case 'UPDATE_QUANTITY': { const { id, quantity } = action.payload; if (quantity i.id !== id) }; } return { ...state, items: state.items.map(i => i.id === id ? { ...i, quantity } : i) }; } case 'CLEAR_CART': return { ...initialState }; case 'APPLY_COUPON': return { ...state, couponCode: action.payload.code, discount: action.payload.discount }; case 'SET_SHIPPING': return { ...state, shippingAddress: action.payload }; case 'SET_PAYMENT': return { ...state, paymentMethod: action.payload }; case 'SET_LOADING': return { ...state, loading: action.payload }; case 'SET_ERROR': return { ...state, error: action.payload }; default: return state; } } export function CartProvider({ children }) { const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(cartReducer, initialState, init => { try { const saved = localStorage.getItem('cart-state'); return saved ? { ...init, ...JSON.parse(saved) } : init; } catch { return init; } }); useEffect(() => { localStorage.setItem('cart-state', JSON.stringify(state)); }, [state]); const addItem = (product, quantity = 1, variant = null) => { dispatch({ type: 'ADD_ITEM', payload: { id: product.id, name: product.name, price: product.price, image: product.images[0], variant: variant || product.defaultVariant, quantity, maxQuantity: product.stock } }); }; const updateQuantity = (id, quantity) => { dispatch({ type: 'UPDATE_QUANTITY', payload: { id, quantity } }); }; const removeItem = (id) => { dispatch({ type: 'REMOVE_ITEM', payload: id }); }; const clearCart = () => dispatch({ type: 'CLEAR_CART' }); const cartTotal = state.items.reduce( (sum, item) => sum + item.price * item.quantity, 0 ); const cartCount = state.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.quantity, 0); const shipping = cartTotal >= 50 ? 0 : 5.99; const tax = cartTotal * 0.08; const total = cartTotal + shipping + tax - state.discount; return ( {children} ); } Step 2: Stripe Payment Integration Integrate Stripe Elements for secure payment collection. Create a PaymentIntent on the server and handle the client-side confirmation with 3D Secure support. import { Elements, CardElement, useStripe, useElements } from '@stripe/react-stripe-js'; import { loadStripe } from '@stripe/stripe-js'; const stripePromise = loadStripe(process.env.REACT_APP_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY); function CheckoutForm({ amount, onSuccess }) { const stripe = useStripe(); const elements = useElements(); const [processing, setProcessing] = useState(false); const [error, setError] = useState(null); const handleSubmit = async (e) => { e.preventDefault(); if (!stripe || !elements) return; setProcessing(true); setError(null); try { const response = await fetch('/api/checkout/create-payment-intent', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ amount: Math.round(amount * 100) }) }); const { clientSecret } = await response.json(); const { error: stripeError, paymentIntent } = await stripe.confirmCardPayment( clientSecret, { payment_method: { card: elements.getElement(CardElement), billing_details: { name: 'Customer Name' } } } ); if (stripeError) { setError(stripeError.message); setProcessing(false); return; } if (paymentIntent.status === 'succeeded') { onSuccess(paymentIntent.id); } } catch (err) { setError('Payment failed. Please try again.'); setProcessing(false); } }; return ( {error && {error} } {processing ? 'Processing...' : `Pay $${amount.toFixed(2)}`} ); } // Server-side: PaymentIntent creation app.post('/api/checkout/create-payment-intent', async (req, res) => { const { amount } = req.body; try { const paymentIntent = await stripe.paymentIntents.create({ amount, currency: 'usd', automatic_payment_methods: { enabled: true }, metadata: { integration_check: 'accept_a_payment' } }); res.json({ clientSecret: paymentIntent.client_secret }); } catch (err) { res.status(500).json({ error: err.message }); } }); Step 3: Product Filtering with URL Params Implement a URL-based filtering system where filter state is stored in URL query parameters, enabling shareable filtered links and browser back/forward navigation. import { useSearchParams } from 'react-router-dom'; function useProductFilters(products) { const [searchParams, setSearchParams] = useSearchParams(); const filters = { category: searchParams.get('category') || 'all', minPrice: Number(searchParams.get('minPrice')) || 0, maxPrice: Number(searchParams.get('maxPrice')) || Infinity, rating: Number(searchParams.get('rating')) || 0, sort: searchParams.get('sort') || 'newest', search: searchParams.get('search') || '', inStock: searchParams.get('inStock') === 'true' }; const setFilter = (key, value) => { setSearchParams(prev => { if (value === null || value === '' || value === 'all' || value === 0) { prev.delete(key); } else { prev.set(key, value); } return prev; }); }; const clearFilters = () => setSearchParams({}); const filtered = useMemo(() => { let result = [...products]; if (filters.category !== 'all') { result = result.filter(p => p.category === filters.category); } result = result.filter(p => p.price >= filters.minPrice && p.price 0) { result = result.filter(p => p.rating >= filters.rating); } if (filters.search) { const q = filters.search.toLowerCase(); result = result.filter(p => p.name.toLowerCase().includes(q) || p.description.toLowerCase().includes(q) || p.tags.some(t => t.toLowerCase().includes(q)) ); } if (filters.inStock) { result = result.filter(p => p.stock > 0); } switch (filters.sort) { case 'price-asc': result.sort((a, b) => a.price - b.price); break; case 'price-desc': result.sort((a, b) => b.price - a.price); break; case 'rating': result.sort((a, b) => b.rating - a.rating); break; case 'name': result.sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name)); break; default: result.sort((a, b) => new Date(b.createdAt) - new Date(a.createdAt)); break; } return result; }, [products, filters]); const categories = useMemo(() => [...new Set(products.map(p => p.category))], [products] ); return { filters, setFilter, clearFilters, filtered, categories }; } Step 4: Multi-step Checkout Build a multi-step checkout form with React state management. Each step validates before advancing. The wizard pattern preserves data across steps and allows going back to edit. const STEPS = ['Shipping', 'Payment', 'Review']; function Checkout() { const [step, setStep] = useState(0); const [formData, setFormData] = useState({ shipping: { firstName: '', lastName: '', address: '', city: '', state: '', zip: '', country: 'US' }, payment: { method: 'card' } }); const [errors, setErrors] = useState({}); const validateStep = (stepIndex) => { const newErrors = {}; if (stepIndex === 0) { const s = formData.shipping; if (!s.firstName.trim()) newErrors.firstName = 'Required'; if (!s.lastName.trim()) newErrors.lastName = 'Required'; if (!s.address.trim()) newErrors.address = 'Required'; if (!s.city.trim()) newErrors.city = 'Required'; if (!s.zip.trim()) newErrors.zip = 'Required'; if (s.zip.length { if (validateStep(step)) setStep(s => Math.min(s + 1, STEPS.length - 1)); }; const prevStep = () => setStep(s => Math.max(s - 1, 0)); const updateShipping = (field, value) => { setFormData(prev => ({ ...prev, shipping: { ...prev.shipping, [field]: value } })); if (errors[field]) setErrors(prev => ({ ...prev, [field]: undefined })); }; const renderStep = () => { switch (step) { case 0: return ; case 1: return setFormData(p => ({ ...p, payment: { ...p.payment, method: m } }))} />; case 2: return ; default: return null; } }; return ( {renderStep()} {step > 0 && Back } {step Continue : } ); }
sections.Testing Guide: Cart Testing: Add items, update quantities, remove items, verify totals update correctly. Test with coupons and discount codes. Clear cart and verify empty state. Test cart persistence across page refreshes. Checkout Testing: Complete the full checkout flow with Stripe test card numbers: 4242424242424242 (success), 4000000000000002 (decline), 4000002500003155 (3D Secure required). Verify error handling for declined cards and network failures. Product Filtering: Apply each filter individually and in combination. Test sorting by each option. Share a filtered URL and verify filters load correctly. Test browser back/forward with filters. Responsive Testing: Test the product grid on mobile (1 column), tablet (2 columns), and desktop (4 columns). Verify the cart drawer works on small screens. Test touch interactions for image galleries.
sections.Deployment Guide: Frontend: Build the React app (npm run build) and deploy the build folder to Vercel, Netlify, or AWS S3 + CloudFront. Configure environment variables in the hosting dashboard. Backend: Deploy the Node.js server to Heroku, Railway, Render, or AWS Elastic Beanstalk. Use PM2 for process management. Set up a MongoDB Atlas cluster or PostgreSQL on RDS. Stripe Webhooks: Register your production webhook endpoint in the Stripe Dashboard. Listen for payment_intent.succeeded, payment_intent.payment_failed, and checkout.session.completed events. Use the Stripe CLI for local webhook testing. Domain & SSL: Set up a custom domain with your DNS provider. Configure SSL certificates through your hosting provider (automatic on Vercel/Netlify). Set up a CDN for product images (Cloudinary, imgix, or CloudFront).
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Insecure Payment Data Handling Never send raw credit card numbers to your server. Stripe Elements encrypts card data client-side and returns a token/payment method ID. Your server only receives this token. PCI DSS compliance requires that card data never touches your server. Always use Stripe's recommended integration pattern and never log payment details. 2. Cart State Desync Between Client and Server When a user adds items to cart on one device, the cart isn't synced to their account. Implement cart merging on login: the server-side cart and client-side cart should merge, preferring server items for products that exist in both. Use the server's cart as the source of truth once authenticated. 3. Missing Inventory Checks During Checkout A user adds 10 items to cart but only 3 are in stock. Without real-time inventory checks, the order will fail after payment. Implement atomic inventory checks during checkout: lock inventory when the checkout process starts, release on timeout if not completed, and decrement on successful payment. Use database transactions for stock operations. 4. Not Handling Payment Webhook Idempotency Stripe webhooks can be delivered multiple times (retry logic). Without idempotency, the same order can be processed twice. Store processed webhook event IDs in a database with a unique constraint. Check for duplicate events before processing. Use the Stripe-Signature header to verify webhook authenticity.
sections.Interview Questions: Q: Explain the difference between Context API and Redux. When would you use each? A: Context API is built into React and is suitable for small to medium applications with simple state (theme, auth, locale). Redux provides a more structured approach with middleware, devtools, time-travel debugging, and normalized state. Use Context for low-frequency updates and simple state. Use Redux for complex state with frequent updates, cross-cutting concerns, or when you need middleware (sagas, thunks). For e-commerce, Context + useReducer works well for cart state, but a global auth store might benefit from Redux if you need complex permission logic. Q: How would you optimize an e-commerce product listing page with 10,000+ products? A: Implement server-side pagination (20-50 products per page) with cursor-based or offset pagination. Use virtual scrolling (react-window or react-virtuoso) for the product grid. Implement server-side filtering and sorting to avoid loading all products. Use GraphQL to fetch only the fields needed (product name, price, image URL). Lazy load product images with Intersection Observer. Cache API responses with React Query or SWR. Prefetch the next page on hover/scroll. Q: Describe the Stripe payment flow from checkout to order confirmation. A: (1) Client collects payment details via Stripe Elements (CardElement). (2) Client sends the total amount to the server. (3) Server creates a PaymentIntent with the amount, currency, and payment_method_types. (4) Server returns the client_secret to the client. (5) Client calls stripe.confirmCardPayment(client_secret) which handles 3D Secure if needed. (6) Stripe processes the payment and returns a result. (7) Server listens for payment_intent.succeeded webhook and updates the order status. (8) Client redirects to the order confirmation page with the payment status. Q: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components in React? A: Controlled components have their state managed by React via useState/useReducer and onChange handlers. The input's value is always driven by React state. Uncontrolled components manage their own DOM state internally, accessed via refs. Controlled components give more control (instant validation, conditional disabling, format on input) but require more code. Uncontrolled are simpler but less flexible. In e-commerce, use controlled components for the checkout form (validation required) and uncontrolled for a search input (less critical). Q: How do you implement optimistic updates in a cart system? A: Optimistic updates immediately apply changes to the UI before the server confirms, creating a faster user experience. Pattern: (1) Update local cart state immediately with the new item. (2) Send the API request to sync with the server. (3) If successful, keep the optimistic state. (4) If failed, rollback to the previous state and show an error toast. Use a snapshot of the previous state for rollback. Example: when adding to cart, immediately increment the cart badge count and add the item to the local cart, then POST to /api/cart/add. On error, revert to the snapshot.
sections.Resume Description: Architected a full-stack e-commerce platform with React 18 frontend using Context API and useReducer for complex cart, auth, and UI state management across 15+ integrated components Integrated Stripe Elements payment processing with PCI-compliant client-side card collection, PaymentIntent server API, 3D Secure authentication, and idempotent webhook handling for order fulfillment Built a multi-step checkout wizard with progressive form validation, shipping calculation, tax estimation, and coupon discount application supporting complex business rules Implemented URL-based product filtering system supporting category, price range, rating, availability, and search parameters with full browser history integration and shareable filtered URLs Developed a Node.js/Express REST API with MongoDB for product catalog, order management, and user authentication with JWT tokens and refresh token rotation Designed responsive product grids with lazy-loaded images, variant selection, zoom functionality, and skeleton loading states optimized for Core Web Vitals Implemented optimistic cart updates with server synchronization, inventory locking during checkout, and cross-device cart merging on user authentication
sections.GitHub Best Practices: README.md: Include architecture diagram, setup instructions for both client and server, environment variable documentation, Stripe test card numbers, and deployment guide. Add "Screenshots" section showing the store on desktop and mobile. Environment Variables: Document all variables in .env.example. Use different .env files for development, staging, and production. Never commit .env files. Use a CI/CD pipeline that injects environment variables during deployment. Commit Messages: feat: add Stripe payment intent creation, fix: prevent duplicate cart items on rapid clicks, refactor: extract checkout steps into custom hook, chore: add Docker configuration for development environment.
sections.Extension Ideas: Admin Dashboard: Build an admin panel with product management (add/edit/delete), order management, inventory tracking, and analytics dashboard. Implement role-based access control with admin and staff roles. Wishlist & Comparisons: Add a wishlist feature with shareable public wishlists. Implement product comparison with a side-by-side view that highlights differences in specifications, prices, and ratings. Internationalization: Add multi-currency support with real-time exchange rate API. Implement multi-language (i18n) with react-intl. Add localized shipping rules, tax calculations, and payment methods based on the user's country. AI Product Recommendations: Implement a recommendation engine using collaborative filtering or matrix factorization. Show "You might also like" on product pages, "Frequently bought together" on cart, and personalized recommendations on the homepage.

description: An intelligent code assistant that uses AI to suggest completions, detect bugs, and refactor code.
title: AI Code Assistant

sections.Project Overview: Build an enterprise-grade AI-powered code assistant that delivers intelligent code completion, real-time bug detection, automated refactoring suggestions, natural language-to-code conversion, and context-aware documentation generation. This project integrates large language models (LLMs) with static code analysis, abstract syntax tree (AST) parsing, and vector database retrieval to create an experience rivaling GitHub Copilot, Amazon CodeWhisperer, and Tabnine. You will architect a full-stack system comprising a Python/FastAPI backend with WebSocket streaming, a React+MobX frontend with Monaco Editor, and a ML serving layer supporting both cloud (OpenAI, Anthropic) and self-hosted (CodeLlama, StarCoder) models. The assistant supports multiple programming languages, learns from user feedback, and provides personalized suggestions through fine-tuned embeddings and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
sections.Problem Statement: Modern software development is characterized by increasing codebase complexity, shrinking delivery timelines, and a growing surface area for bugs and security vulnerabilities. Developers spend approximately 40% of their time searching for information and debugging, while only 20% actually writing new code. Existing tools either provide shallow syntactic analysis (linters) or require expensive manual code reviews. There is no unified system that combines deep semantic understanding of code with real-time interactive assistance. The challenge is to build an AI-powered system that understands code holistically — its syntax, semantics, intent, and surrounding context — and provides actionable, context-aware suggestions that adapt to the developer's coding style, project conventions, and the specific problem domain.
sections.Real World Use Cases: Enterprise IDE Plugin Integrated into JetBrains IntelliJ, VS Code, and Eclipse to provide real-time AI completions for Java, Kotlin, and Python enterprise codebases — reducing boilerplate writing by 60%. Security-First Code Review CI/CD integration that scans every PR for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF) using both static analysis and LLM-based semantic understanding with confidence scoring. Code Learning Platform Educational tool for CS students that explains code in natural language, generates practice exercises from existing codebases, and provides step-by-step refactoring walkthroughs. Legacy Modernization Automated migration assistant that reads legacy COBOL/Fortran code, generates semantically equivalent Python/Go implementations, and produces diff reports for manual verification.
sections.Industry Relevance: The AI-assisted coding tools market is projected to reach $75B by 2028. GitHub Copilot alone has over 1.8 million paid subscribers and generates 46% of new code in supported languages. Companies like Google (Project IDX), Amazon (CodeWhisperer), and Meta (Code Llama) are investing heavily in this space. Key industry trends include: (1) Shift from rule-based linters to ML-powered semantic analysis, (2) Rise of self-hosted open-source models for data sovereignty, (3) Integration of code assistants with project management and documentation tools, (4) Automated test generation and QA, (5) Agentic coding where AI autonomously implements features from ticket descriptions. This project directly maps to the skills required for roles at AI-first companies and positions you at the forefront of the AI-assisted development paradigm.
sections.Learning Outcomes: LLM Integration Architecture: Design and implement multi-model AI systems with fallback strategies, streaming responses, and context window management across OpenAI, Anthropic, and self-hosted models. Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) Parsing: Build language-agnostic code analyzers using tree-sitter and Language Server Protocol (LSP) for deep code understanding across 10+ languages. Vector Databases & RAG: Implement Retrieval-Augmented Generation using Chroma/Pinecone to store and retrieve code embeddings for context-aware suggestions across large codebases. Real-Time WebSocket Architecture: Design bidirectional streaming systems with debouncing, cancellation, reconnection logic, and backpressure handling for sub-200ms latency. Prompt Engineering for Code: Master prompt design patterns specific to code generation, bug detection, refactoring, and explanation — including few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured output formats. Fine-Tuning & Model Customization: Learn to fine-tune CodeLlama and StarCoder on custom codebases using LoRA/QLoRA for domain-specific optimization. Frontend Editor Integration: Integrate Monaco Editor with inline completion providers, decoration APIs, and custom keybindings for seamless UX. CI/CD for ML Systems: Build MLOps pipelines for model evaluation, A/B testing, and gradual rollout of AI features.
sections.Technologies Used: Python 3.11+ 3.11 Core backend and ML orchestration FastAPI 0.110 Async WebSocket + REST API server OpenAI / Anthropic GPT-4o / Claude 3.5 Cloud LLM providers CodeLlama / StarCoder 7B-34B Self-hosted local models tree-sitter 0.21 AST parsing for 10+ languages Chroma / Pinecone 0.5 Vector database for code embeddings React 18 + MobX 18.2 Frontend with state management Monaco Editor 0.44 Code editor with inline completions Docker + CUDA 24.0 Containerized model serving MLflow / Weights & Biases 2.10 Experiment tracking and model registry
sections.Prerequisites: Python Proficiency: Strong Python 3.x skills including asyncio, async/await patterns, decorators, and type hints. Comfortable with dependency management (pip/poetry). Web Frameworks: Experience with FastAPI or Flask for building REST and WebSocket APIs. Understanding of middleware, CORS, rate limiting, and authentication. Reactor Frontend: Solid understanding of React hooks, context, state management (Redux/MobX), and component lifecycle. Familiarity with Monaco or CodeMirror editor integration is a plus. Machine Learning Basics: Understanding of transformer architecture, tokenization, attention mechanisms, embeddings, and fine-tuning concepts. Familiarity with Hugging Face Transformers library. Prompt Engineering: Experience designing prompts for LLMs, understanding of temperature/top-p sampling, few-shot learning, chain-of-thought prompting, and structured output generation. Database Knowledge: Familiarity with both relational (PostgreSQL) and vector (Chroma/Pinecone) databases. Understanding of embeddings and similarity search (cosine similarity, dot product). DevOps Basics: Docker containerization, GPU driver setup, basic CI/CD with GitHub Actions, and cloud deployment (AWS EC2 G5 instances or RunPod).
sections.Database Design: PostgreSQL Schema Table Columns Description users id (UUID PK), email, username, password_hash, created_at, api_keys (JSONB), preferences (JSONB) User accounts and auth projects id (UUID PK), user_id (FK), name, language, root_path, last_opened, file_count, token_count User projects attached to accounts files id (UUID PK), project_id (FK), path, language, content_hash, embedding_id, last_analyzed Individual source files with hash dedup completions id (UUID PK), user_id, file_id, trigger_context (TEXT), suggestion (TEXT), accepted (BOOL), latency_ms, model_used Log of all completions for analytics + fine-tuning bug_reports id (UUID PK), file_id, line_start, line_end, severity, category, description, fix_suggestion, dismissed (BOOL) Static analysis bug detections refactoring_history id (UUID PK), file_id, refactoring_type, original_snippet, refactored_snippet, applied (BOOL), user_rating Refactoring operations log feedback id (UUID PK), user_id, target_type (completion/bug/refactor), target_id, rating (1-5), comment, created_at User feedback for RLHF pipeline code_embeddings id (UUID PK), file_id, chunk_index, chunk_text, embedding (vector(1536)), language, created_at Code chunks with vector embeddings Relationships users 1→* projects → files → code_embeddings — A user has many projects, each project has many files, each file can be chunked into multiple embeddings. The completions , bug_reports , and refactoring_history tables all reference files and form the feedback loop. The feedback table uses polymorphic associations via target_type + target_id .
sections.API Structure: Endpoint Method Description Request Response /ws/completion WebSocket Real-time code completion stream {code, cursor, language, file_id} Stream: {type, content, request_id} /ws/analyze WebSocket Real-time static analysis stream {code, language, file_id} Stream: {type, bugs[], suggestions[]} /api/chat POST Natural language code queries {message, context, language} {response, code_snippets[]} /api/refactor POST Get refactoring suggestions {code, language, focus_area} {refactorings[], diff} /api/apply-refactoring POST Apply a specific refactoring {file_id, refactoring_id} {new_code, diff} /api/projects CRUD Manage user projects Project data Project object /api/files CRUD Manage files within project File data File object /api/feedback POST Submit user feedback {target_type, target_id, rating, comment} {status} /api/models GET List available AI models - {models[{id, name, provider, latency}]} /api/fine-tune POST Trigger fine-tuning job {project_id, model, hyperparams} {job_id, status} /api/embedding/search POST Search similar code snippets {query, language, limit} {results[{file, snippet, score}]}
