sections.Implementation Guide:
Step 1: Multipart Upload with Presigned URLs // upload-service/src/routes/upload.ts import { Router } from 'express'; import { S3Client, CreateMultipartUploadCommand, UploadPartCommand, CompleteMultipartUploadCommand, AbortMultipartUploadCommand } from '@aws-sdk/client-s3'; import { getSignedUrl } from '@aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner'; const router = Router(); const s3 = new S3Client({ region: process.env.AWS_REGION }); const BUCKET = process.env.RAW_VIDEO_BUCKET!; // Initiate multipart upload router.post('/upload/initiate', async (req, res) => { const { fileName, fileSize, mimeType } = req.body; const userId = req.user.id; // Create S3 multipart upload const command = new CreateMultipartUploadCommand({ Bucket: BUCKET, Key: `uploads/${userId}/${Date.now()}-${fileName}`, ContentType: mimeType, Metadata: { userId, originalName: fileName, fileSize: String(fileSize) }, }); const { UploadId, Key } = await s3.send(command); // Generate presigned URLs for each part const partCount = Math.ceil(fileSize / (5 * 1024 * 1024)); // 5MB parts const presignedUrls = await Promise.all( Array.from({ length: partCount }, (_, i) => getSignedUrl(s3, new UploadPartCommand({ Bucket: BUCKET, Key, UploadId, PartNumber: i + 1, }), { expiresIn: 3600 }) ) ); res.json({ uploadId: UploadId, key: Key, presignedUrls, partCount, }); }); // Complete multipart upload router.post('/upload/complete', async (req, res) => { const { key, uploadId, parts } = req.body; const command = new CompleteMultipartUploadCommand({ Bucket: BUCKET, Key: key, UploadId: uploadId, MultipartUpload: { Parts: parts.map((p: any) => ({ ETag: p.etag, PartNumber: p.partNumber })) }, }); const result = await s3.send(command); // Trigger transcoding pipeline await fetch(`${process.env.API_URL}/api/videos/process`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ key: result.Key, bucket: BUCKET }), }); res.json({ location: result.Location, key: result.Key }); }); // Abort multipart upload router.post('/upload/abort', async (req, res) => { const { key, uploadId } = req.body; await s3.send(new AbortMultipartUploadCommand({ Bucket: BUCKET, Key: key, UploadId: uploadId, })); res.json({ status: 'aborted' }); }); export default router; Step 2: FFmpeg Transcoding Pipeline // transcode-worker/src/services/ffmpeg.ts import { spawn } from 'child_process'; import { S3Client, GetObjectCommand, PutObjectCommand } from '@aws-sdk/client-s3'; import { Readable } from 'stream'; import { pipeline } from 'stream/promises'; interface TranscodeConfig { inputKey: string; outputPrefix: string; bucket: string; resolutions: Resolution[]; } interface Resolution { name: string; // '1080p', '720p', etc. width: number; height: number; bitrate: string; // '5000k', '2500k', etc. maxrate: string; bufsize: string; } const RESOLUTIONS: Resolution[] = [ { name: '1080p', width: 1920, height: 1080, bitrate: '5000k', maxrate: '7500k', bufsize: '10000k' }, { name: '720p', width: 1280, height: 720, bitrate: '2500k', maxrate: '3750k', bufsize: '5000k' }, { name: '480p', width: 854, height: 480, bitrate: '1000k', maxrate: '1500k', bufsize: '2000k' }, { name: '360p', width: 640, height: 360, bitrate: '600k', maxrate: '900k', bufsize: '1200k' }, ]; export async function transcodeVideo(config: TranscodeConfig): Promise { const s3 = new S3Client({ region: process.env.AWS_REGION }); // Download from S3 const getCommand = new GetObjectCommand({ Bucket: config.bucket, Key: config.inputKey, }); const response = await s3.send(getCommand); const inputStream = response.Body as Readable; // Transcode each resolution const variantPlaylists: string[] = []; for (const resolution of config.resolutions) { const outputKey = `${config.outputPrefix}/${resolution.name}/index.m3u8`; const segmentPattern = `${config.outputPrefix}/${resolution.name}/segment_%04d.ts`; const ffmpegArgs = [ '-i', 'pipe:0', '-vf', `scale=w=${resolution.width}:h=${resolution.height}:force_original_aspect_ratio=decrease,pad=${resolution.width}:${resolution.height}:(ow-iw)/2:(oh-ih)/2`, '-c:v', 'libx264', '-b:v', resolution.bitrate, '-maxrate', resolution.maxrate, '-bufsize', resolution.bufsize, '-preset', 'medium', '-crf', '23', '-c:a', 'aac', '-b:a', '128k', '-ar', '48000', '-f', 'hls', '-hls_time', '6', '-hls_list_size', '0', '-hls_segment_filename', segmentPattern, '-hls_playlist_type', 'vod', '-movflags', '+faststart', '-progress', 'pipe:2', '-y', outputKey, ]; await new Promise ((resolve, reject) => { const ffmpeg = spawn('ffmpeg', ffmpegArgs, { stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'] }); // Pipe S3 stream to ffmpeg pipeline(inputStream, ffmpeg.stdin!).catch(reject); let stderr = ''; ffmpeg.stderr!.on('data', (data) => { stderr += data.toString(); }); ffmpeg.on('close', (code) => { if (code === 0) { variantPlaylists.push(`#EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=${parseInt(resolution.bitrate) * 1000},RESOLUTION=${resolution.width}x${resolution.height},CODECS="avc1.640028,mp4a.40.2"
${resolution.name}/index.m3u8`); resolve(); } else { reject(new Error(`FFmpeg exited with code ${code}: ${stderr}`)); } }); }); } // Generate master playlist const masterPlaylist = [ '#EXTM3U', '#EXT-X-VERSION:3', ...variantPlaylists, ].join('
'); const masterKey = `${config.outputPrefix}/master.m3u8`; await s3.send(new PutObjectCommand({ Bucket: config.bucket, Key: masterKey, Body: masterPlaylist, ContentType: 'application/vnd.apple.mpegurl', })); return masterKey; } Step 3: HLS Player with Adaptive Bitrate // frontend/src/components/VideoPlayer.tsx import React, { useEffect, useRef, useState } from 'react'; import Hls from 'hls.js'; interface VideoPlayerProps { src: string; // Master playlist URL poster?: string; autoQuality?: boolean; onProgress?: (currentTime: number, duration: number) => void; onQualityChange?: (quality: { height: number; bitrate: number }) => void; } export const VideoPlayer: React.FC = ({ src, poster, autoQuality = true, onProgress, onQualityChange, }) => { const videoRef = useRef (null); const hlsRef = useRef (null); const [qualities, setQualities] = useState ([]); const [currentQuality, setCurrentQuality] = useState (-1); // -1 = auto const [isPlaying, setIsPlaying] = useState(false); const [buffered, setBuffered] = useState(0); const progressInterval = useRef (); useEffect(() => { const video = videoRef.current; if (!video) return; if (Hls.isSupported()) { const hls = new Hls({ enableWorker: true, lowLatencyMode: true, backbufferLength: 30, maxBufferLength: 30, maxMaxBufferLength: 60, startLevel: currentQuality === -1 ? -1 : currentQuality, abrEwmaFastVoD: 3, abrEwmaSlowVoD: 8, abrBandWidthUpFactor: 0.7, abrBandWidthDownFactor: 0.4, }); hls.loadSource(src); hls.attachMedia(video); hlsRef.current = hls; hls.on(Hls.Events.MANIFEST_PARSED, (event, data) => { const qualityLevels = data.levels.map((level, index) => ({ index, height: level.height, bitrate: level.bitrate, name: level.height >= 1440 ? '4K' : level.height >= 1080 ? '1080p' : level.height >= 720 ? '720p' : level.height >= 480 ? '480p' : '360p', })); setQualities(qualityLevels); }); hls.on(Hls.Events.LEVEL_SWITCHED, (event, data) => { const level = hls.levels[data.level]; onQualityChange?.({ height: level.height, bitrate: level.bitrate }); }); hls.on(Hls.Events.ERROR, (event, data) => { if (data.fatal) { switch (data.type) { case Hls.ErrorTypes.NETWORK_ERROR: hls.startLoad(); break; case Hls.ErrorTypes.MEDIA_ERROR: hls.recoverMediaError(); break; default: break; } } }); } else if (video.canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl')) { // Native HLS support (Safari) video.src = src; } // Progress tracking progressInterval.current = window.setInterval(() => { if (video && !video.paused) { onProgress?.(video.currentTime, video.duration); // Buffered percentage if (video.buffered.length > 0) { setBuffered(video.buffered.end(video.buffered.length - 1) / video.duration); } } }, 1000); return () => { hlsRef.current?.destroy(); clearInterval(progressInterval.current); }; }, [src]); const switchQuality = (levelIndex: number) => { if (hlsRef.current) { hlsRef.current.currentLevel = levelIndex; setCurrentQuality(levelIndex); } }; const togglePlay = () => { const video = videoRef.current; if (!video) return; if (video.paused) { video.play(); setIsPlaying(true); } else { video.pause(); setIsPlaying(false); } }; return ( {/* Quality selector */} switchQuality(parseInt(e.target.value))} style={{ background: 'rgba(0,0,0,0.8)', color: 'white', border: '1px solid #444', padding: '4px 8px', borderRadius: '4px', fontSize: '12px', }} > Auto {qualities.map((q) => ( {q.name} ({Math.round(q.bitrate / 1000)} Mbps) ))} {/* Custom buffering bar */} ); }; Step 4: Analytics Pipeline // analytics-pipeline/src/consumers/watchEvent.ts import { Kafka } from 'kafkajs'; import { ClickHouse } from 'clickhouse'; const kafka = new Kafka({ clientId: 'analytics-consumer', brokers: process.env.KAFKA_BROKERS!.split(','), }); const clickhouse = new ClickHouse({ host: process.env.CLICKHOUSE_HOST, port: 8123, protocol: 'http', }); interface WatchEvent { userId: string; videoId: string; eventType: 'play' | 'pause' | 'seek' | 'quality_change' | 'heartbeat' | 'complete'; timestamp: number; currentTime: number; duration: number; quality: { height: number; bitrate: number }; deviceType: string; ipAddress: string; userAgent: string; sessionId: string; } const consumer = kafka.consumer({ groupId: 'watch-events-group' }); async function startConsumer() { await consumer.connect(); await consumer.subscribe({ topic: 'video.watch.events', fromBeginning: true }); await consumer.run({ eachBatch: async ({ batch, resolveOffset, heartbeat }) => { const events: WatchEvent[] = batch.messages .map(msg => { try { return JSON.parse(msg.value!.toString()) as WatchEvent; } catch { return null; } }) .filter((e): e is WatchEvent => e !== null); if (events.length === 0) return; // Batch insert into ClickHouse const values = events.map(e => { const date = new Date(e.timestamp); return `( '${e.userId}', '${e.videoId}', '${e.eventType}', ${e.currentTime}, ${e.duration}, ${e.quality.height}, ${e.quality.bitrate}, '${e.deviceType}', '${e.ipAddress}', '${e.sessionId}', toDateTime('${date.toISOString().slice(0, 19).replace('T', ' ')}') )`; }).join(',
'); await clickhouse.query(` INSERT INTO watch_events (userId, videoId, eventType, currentTime, duration, qualityHeight, qualityBitrate, deviceType, ipAddress, sessionId, timestamp) VALUES ${values} `).toPromise(); // Resolve all offsets for (const msg of batch.messages) { await resolveOffset(msg.offset); } await heartbeat(); }, }); } // Daily aggregation job async function runDailyAggregation() { const yesterday = new Date(Date.now() - 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString().slice(0, 10); await clickhouse.query(` INSERT INTO analytics_daily (date, videoId, views, uniqueViewers, watchTimeMinutes, avgWatchPercentage, completionRate) SELECT toDate(timestamp) as date, videoId, countDistinct(sessionId) as views, uniqExact(userId) as uniqueViewers, sum(if(eventType = 'heartbeat', 10, 0)) / 60 as watchTimeMinutes, avg(currentTime / duration) * 100 as avgWatchPercentage, countIf(eventType = 'complete') / countDistinct(sessionId) * 100 as completionRate FROM watch_events WHERE toDate(timestamp) = '${yesterday}' GROUP BY date, videoId `).toPromise(); }
sections.Video Encoding Pipelines: FFmpeg Pipeline: Raw video → (1) Detect codec and container, (2) Generate keyframe index for seeking, (3) Transcode to H.264/AAC for maximum compatibility, (4) Generate multiple renditions at 1080p/720p/480p/360p, (5) Segment into 6-second HLS segments, (6) Generate master playlist and variant playlists, (7) Extract thumbnail at 25%, 50%, 75% positions, (8) Generate sprite sheet for timeline preview. The pipeline runs on ECS Fargate with GPU support for faster encoding. For 4K content, we also generate a 4K rendition using HEVC (H.265) codec for 50% bitrate savings.
sections.CDN Concepts (CloudFront, Cloudflare): CloudFront Distribution: (1) Origin: S3 bucket with HLS content, (2) Behavior: /hls/* → Cached for 1 year (versioned content), /thumbnails/* → Cached 1 day, /api/* → Proxy to API Gateway, (3) Origin Shield: Reduces S3 request cost by consolidating requests at regional edge, (4) Price Class: Use Price Class 100 (US, Europe) for cost savings, (5) Lambda@Edge: For token authorization on premium content, (6) WAF: Block malicious requests, geo-restriction for licensed content.
sections.Video Storage Strategies (S3, GCS): Tiered Storage: (1) Hot tier (S3 Standard): Videos published within 30 days, popular content. (2) Warm tier (S3 Infrequent Access): Videos 30-90 days old, moderate access. (3) Cold tier (S3 Glacier Deep Archive): Videos older than 90 days, archival. Lifecycle policies automatically transition objects. For originals (raw uploads), delete after successful transcoding or move to Glacier immediately.
sections.Streaming Protocols (HLS, RTMP, MPEG-DAS: HLS (HTTP Live Streaming): Apple's protocol, widest support. Uses .ts segments with .m3u8 playlists. Supports adaptive bitrate, captions, and DRM (FairPlay). Our primary delivery format. MPEG-DASH: International standard (ISO/IEC 23009-1). Uses .m4s segments with .mpd manifest. Better codec support (VP9, AV1) and more flexible adaptation sets. Used for Android and smart TV apps. RTMP (Real-Time Messaging Protocol): Legacy protocol for live streaming ingest. We receive RTMP from OBS/Streamlabs, then repackage to HLS for delivery. Being replaced by SRT (Secure Reliable Transport) and WHIP (WebRTC HTTP Ingest Protocol).
sections.Adaptive Bitrate Streaming: ABR algorithms dynamically select the best quality based on network conditions. Our implementation (using HLS.js built-in ABR): (1) Measures download bandwidth using xhr progress events, (2) Uses EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) to smooth bandwidth estimates, (3) Selects highest quality that can be sustained with 20% headroom, (4) On buffer depletion, immediately switch down, (5) On stable high bandwidth for 10 seconds, switch up, (6) Configurable: start with lower quality for fast startup (startLevel = -1 = auto), (7) User can override to fixed quality via quality selector.
sections.Transcoding Workflows: Workflow: (1) S3 event notification triggers SQS message when raw upload completes. (2) Transcode worker picks up message, downloads video metadata (duration, codec, resolution). (3) Determines optimal encoding ladder based on source resolution and bitrate. (4) Runs FFmpeg pipeline — outputs HLS segments to temporary storage. (5) Uploads segments to S3 HLS bucket with appropriate cache headers. (6) Generates thumbnail strip (10 screenshots evenly spaced). (7) Updates video status in PostgreSQL to 'ready'. (8) Invalidates CloudFront cache for the video's CDN path. (9) Optionally deletes raw upload.
sections.Analytics Pipeline: Watch events are sent from the video player to a Kafka topic every 10 seconds (heartbeat). Events include: play, pause, seek, quality_change, heartbeat, complete. Kafka consumer batch-inserts into ClickHouse (columnar analytics DB) every second. Daily aggregation jobs produce per-video and per-creator analytics. Dashboards show: (1) Views over time, (2) Audience retention (watch percentage per timestamp), (3) Geographic distribution (CloudFront logs), (4) Device breakdown, (5) Quality distribution, (6) Revenue per video, (7) Subscriber growth.
sections.Subscription/Billing Integration: Stripe integration: (1) Creator subscription tiers: Basic ($4.99/mo), Premium ($9.99/mo), Enterprise ($19.99/mo). (2) Webhook handling for subscription lifecycle (created, updated, canceled, past_due). (3) Per-viewer entitlements checked via middleware on video access. (4) Revenue sharing: 70% to creator, 30% platform fee. (5) Payouts via Stripe Connect to creator bank accounts. (6) Free tier: ads before video (VAST/VPAID ad server integration).
sections.Testing Guide: # Test transcoding with sample video ffmpeg -f lavfi -i testsrc=duration=30:size=1920x1080:rate=30 test-1080p.mp4 # Run transcoding pipeline docker-compose run transcode-worker \ --input test-1080p.mp4 \ --resolutions 1080p,720p,480p # Verify HLS output ls -la output/*/index.m3u8 ffprobe output/1080p/segment_0000.ts # Test HLS playback with ffplay ffplay https://d1jmyj3k6s9c6.cloudfront.net/hls/video123/master.m3u8 # Load test video delivery k6 run scripts/loadtest.js \ -e TARGET_URL=https://d1jmyj3k6s9c6.cloudfront.net/hls/video123/720p/segment_0000.ts
sections.Security Considerations: Signed URLs: All video content served via CloudFront signed URLs with expiration (24h for regular viewing, 7d for offline download). Tokens are tied to user sessions. DRM: Widevine (Android/Chrome), FairPlay (iOS/Safari), and PlayReady (Xbox/Edge) integration using AWS MediaConvert for encrypting content. Upload Validation: Validate file type, scan for malware (ClamAV Lambda), check duration limits (free: 15min, premium: 4h), size limits (free: 1GB, premium: 10GB). Content Moderation: Automated moderation using AWS Rekognition for NSFW content. Manual review queue for flagged videos. Age restriction for mature content.
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Using a single bitrate: Without adaptive streaming, viewers on slow connections buffer endlessly while fast connections get lower quality than possible. Always generate multiple renditions (at least 3: 480p, 720p, 1080p). 2. Not optimizing for mobile: Mobile networks have higher latency and variable bandwidth. Use segment duration of 4s for live (lower latency) and 6s for VOD (better compression). Implement mobile-specific ABR parameters. 3. Ignoring CDN cache hit ratio: Without proper cache headers, every request goes to origin. Set Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 for HLS segments (they're immutable). Use origin shield to reduce S3 costs. 4. No failover for live streams: Live streaming must handle encoder disconnection, network issues, and server failure. Implement auto-reconnect with sequence number continuity. Use redundant ingest endpoints (primary + backup RTMP URLs). 5. Storing all renditions permanently: Storage costs for multiple renditions add up fast. Delete low-bitrate renditions for old/unpopular content after 30 days. Regenerate on-demand if needed.
sections.Advanced Challenges: Challenge 1: Serverless Transcoding Pipeline Replace ECS-based transcoding with AWS Lambda + MediaConvert. Process videos up to 1 hour in Lambda (use EFS for temporary storage, or stream directly). Compare cost and performance vs EC2-based transcoding. Challenge 2: Real-Time Content Moderation Build a moderation pipeline using FFmpeg scene detection + AWS Rekognition. Automatically flag content containing: weapons, violence, adult content, or copyrighted material. Implement human review queue with justification requirement for takedowns. Challenge 3: Peer-Assisted Delivery (P2P CDN) Implement WebRTC-based peer-to-peer video delivery to reduce CDN costs. Use WebTorrent or custom WebRTC mesh for popular content. Serve 30% of traffic via peers. Handle peer churn and NAT traversal. Challenge 4: AI-Powered Video Search Implement semantic video search using CLIP embeddings. Generate embeddings for each video frame at key intervals. Allow natural language search: "show me when the presenter talks about databases". Build a search interface with timestamp results and preview thumbnails. Challenge 5: Multi-CDN Load Balancing Route traffic across CloudFront, Cloudflare, and Fastly based on real-time performance metrics. Implement client-side CDN selector using JavaScript that measures latency to each CDN's probe URL. Failover to backup CDN within 5 seconds. description: Build a fully functional calculator with support for basic arithmetic operations and clean UI.
title: Calculator

sections.Project Overview: This calculator project goes far beyond a basic arithmetic tool. You will build a scientific-grade calculator featuring a multi-line expression display, operator precedence parsing, keyboard shortcuts, an interactive calculation history panel, and a theme toggle. You will learn advanced DOM manipulation techniques, event delegation patterns, expression parsing algorithms (Shunting Yard), and state management. The calculator supports basic operations, percentages, square roots, exponents, parentheses, and memory functions (MC, MR, M+, M-).
sections.Problem Statement: Most calculator tutorials teach building a calculator that evaluates expressions using `eval()`, which is dangerous and insecure. Developers need to understand how to safely parse and evaluate mathematical expressions, handle operator precedence, manage state across operations, and build an intuitive UI that works with both mouse and keyboard input. The challenge is creating a calculator that handles edge cases like division by zero, consecutive operators, decimal precision, and large numbers while providing a smooth, native-app-like user experience.
sections.Real World Use Cases: Everyday Calculations: A student uses the calculator for homework, switching between basic and scientific modes for algebra and trigonometry problems. Financial Planning: A user tracks expenses with running totals, uses memory functions to store recurring amounts, and reviews the calculation history at the end of the month. Engineering Estimates: An engineer performs quick unit conversions and formula calculations, using the expression history to verify previous results without re-entering data.
sections.Industry Relevance: Expression parsing is a fundamental computer science concept used in compilers, interpreters, spreadsheet software, and formula editors. The Shunting Yard algorithm you'll implement was developed by Edsger Dijkstra and is still used in modern applications like Excel, Google Sheets, and Wolfram Alpha. Event delegation patterns are critical for building performant web applications with many interactive elements. Keyboard shortcut handling is essential for power users and accessibility compliance.
sections.Learning Outcomes: Implement the Shunting Yard algorithm for converting infix expressions to postfix (RPN) notation for safe evaluation Build a robust expression parser that handles operator precedence, parentheses, functions (sqrt, pow, sin, cos), and constants (PI, E) Use event delegation pattern to handle all button clicks with a single event listener on the parent container Implement full keyboard support with keydown/keyup event handling for numbers, operators, Enter/Escape, and backspace Manage calculator state including current expression, history, memory, and theme using a state machine pattern Handle floating-point precision issues using Number.EPSILON comparisons and toFixed for display purposes Build an animated calculation history panel with localStorage persistence and the ability to reuse previous results Implement CSS custom property theming for light/dark mode with smooth transitions on all interactive elements
sections.Technologies Used: JavaScript ES6+ HTML5 CSS3 localStorage Font Awesome
sections.Prerequisites: Proficient in JavaScript fundamentals: functions, objects, arrays, string manipulation Understanding of event listeners and event object properties (key, target, type) Basic knowledge of CSS Grid for button layout and CSS transitions for animations Familiarity with recursion and stack data structures (helpful but not required) Understanding of floating-point arithmetic limitations in binary systems
sections.Architecture Overview: The calculator follows a state machine architecture with three main states: INPUT (user is typing an expression), EVALUATE (expression is being computed), and DISPLAY (result is shown). The core evaluation engine uses the Shunting Yard algorithm: the infix expression is tokenized, converted to Reverse Polish Notation using a stack-based algorithm, then evaluated using another stack. Button clicks are handled through a single event delegation listener on the calculator body, with each button's data-action attribute determining the behavior. Keyboard events are normalized to the same action map. The history is stored as an array of { expression, result, timestamp } objects in localStorage with a maximum of 50 entries.
sections.Folder Structure: calculator/ ├── index.html ├── css/ │ └── style.css ├── js/ │ ├── app.js │ ├── calculator.js │ ├── parser.js │ ├── history.js │ └── theme.js ├── assets/ │ └── icons/ ├── .gitignore └── README.md
sections.Development Roadmap: Phase 1: Button Layout & Event Delegation Build the calculator grid layout with CSS Grid (4 columns for basic, expand to more for scientific mode). Implement event delegation on a single container element. Each button has a data-action attribute (number, operator, function, memory, clear, equals, backspace, decimal). Phase 2: Expression Builder Build the expression builder that handles number input, decimal points (prevent multiple dots in one number), operator chaining, parentheses auto-completion, and display formatting with commas. Manage cursor position for editing. Phase 3: Expression Parser & Evaluator Implement the tokenizer that splits the expression into numbers, operators, functions, and parentheses. Implement the Shunting Yard algorithm. Implement the RPN evaluator. Handle error cases: division by zero, invalid expressions, overflow, and NaN results. Phase 4: Keyboard Support Map keyboard events to calculator actions. Numbers and operators type directly. Enter = equals, Escape = clear, Backspace = delete last character, Delete = clear all. Shift+( = open paren, Shift+) = close paren. Percentage sign, asterisk for multiply, slash for divide. Phase 5: History & Theme Build the history panel that slides in from the right or appears as a dropdown. Each history item shows the expression, result, and timestamp. Click to reuse a result. Store history in localStorage. Add theme toggle with CSS custom properties. Persist theme preference.
sections.Implementation Guide: Step 1: Shunting Yard Algorithm Implementation The Shunting Yard algorithm converts human-readable infix expressions (e.g., "3 + 4 * 2") to postfix notation ("3 4 2 * +") which is easy to evaluate with a stack. This avoids the security risks of eval(). const PRECEDENCE = { '+': 2, '-': 2, '*': 3, '/': 3, '%': 3, '^': 4, 'sqrt': 4, 'sin': 5, 'cos': 5, 'tan': 5 }; const ASSOCIATIVITY = { '+': 'L', '-': 'L', '*': 'L', '/': 'L', '%': 'L', '^': 'R', 'sqrt': 'R' }; function tokenize(expr) { const tokens = []; let i = 0; while (i 0 && opStack[opStack.length - 1].value !== '(') { output.push(opStack.pop()); } if (opStack.length === 0) throw new Error('Mismatched parentheses'); opStack.pop(); if (opStack.length > 0 && opStack[opStack.length - 1].type === 'function') { output.push(opStack.pop()); } } else if (token.type === 'operator') { while ( opStack.length > 0 && opStack[opStack.length - 1].type !== 'function' && opStack[opStack.length - 1].value !== '(' && (PRECEDENCE[opStack[opStack.length - 1].value] > PRECEDENCE[token.value] || (PRECEDENCE[opStack[opStack.length - 1].value] === PRECEDENCE[token.value] && ASSOCIATIVITY[token.value] === 'L')) ) { output.push(opStack.pop()); } opStack.push(token); } } while (opStack.length > 0) { const op = opStack.pop(); if (op.value === '(' || op.value === ')') throw new Error('Mismatched parentheses'); output.push(op); } return output; } function evaluateRPN(tokens) { const stack = []; for (const token of tokens) { if (token.type === 'number') { stack.push(token.value); } else if (token.type === 'unary') { const a = stack.pop(); if (a === undefined) throw new Error('Insufficient operands'); stack.push(-a); } else if (token.type === 'function') { const a = stack.pop(); if (a === undefined) throw new Error('Insufficient operands'); switch (token.value) { case 'sqrt': stack.push(Math.sqrt(a)); break; case 'sin': stack.push(Math.sin(a)); break; case 'cos': stack.push(Math.cos(a)); break; case 'tan': stack.push(Math.tan(a)); break; case 'abs': stack.push(Math.abs(a)); break; case 'round': stack.push(Math.round(a)); break; case 'floor': stack.push(Math.floor(a)); break; case 'ceil': stack.push(Math.ceil(a)); break; default: throw new Error('Unknown function: ' + token.value); } } else if (token.type === 'operator') { const b = stack.pop(); const a = stack.pop(); if (a === undefined || b === undefined) throw new Error('Insufficient operands'); switch (token.value) { case '+': stack.push(a + b); break; case '-': stack.push(a - b); break; case '*': stack.push(a * b); break; case '/': if (Math.abs(b) Step 2: Event Delegation for Button Grid Use a single event listener on the calculator container to handle all button clicks. Each button's data-action attribute determines the handler. const calculator = document.getElementById('calculator'); const actions = { number: (value) => appendNumber(value), operator: (value) => appendOperator(value), decimal: () => appendDecimal(), clear: () => clearAll(), backspace: () => deleteLast(), equals: () => calculate(), percent: () => percent(), negate: () => negate(), memory_clear: () => memoryClear(), memory_recall: () => memoryRecall(), memory_add: () => memoryAdd(), memory_subtract: () => memorySubtract(), sqrt: () => appendFunction('sqrt('), square: () => appendOperator('^').then(() => appendNumber(2)), reciprocal: () => calculate().then(() => appendOperator('/').then(() => appendNumber(1))), history: () => toggleHistory(), theme: () => toggleTheme() }; calculator.addEventListener('click', (e) => { e.stopPropagation(); const button = e.target.closest('.calc-btn'); if (!button || button.disabled) return; const action = button.dataset.action; const value = button.dataset.value; if (actions[action]) { actions[action](value); updateDisplay(); buttonFeedback(button); } }); function buttonFeedback(btn) { btn.classList.add('pressed'); setTimeout(() => btn.classList.remove('pressed'), 100); } Step 3: Keyboard Support Mapping Map keyboard events to the same action system used by button clicks. Handle both standard keys and numpad keys. const keyMap = { '0': ['number', '0'], '1': ['number', '1'], '2': ['number', '2'], '3': ['number', '3'], '4': ['number', '4'], '5': ['number', '5'], '6': ['number', '6'], '7': ['number', '7'], '8': ['number', '8'], '9': ['number', '9'], '.': ['decimal'], ',': ['decimal'], '+': ['operator', '+'], '-': ['operator', '-'], '*': ['operator', '*'], '/': ['operator', '/'], '%': ['percent'], '^': ['operator', '^'], '(': ['paren', '('], ')': ['paren', ')'], 'Enter': ['equals'], '=': ['equals'], 'Escape': ['clear'], 'Backspace': ['backspace'], 'Delete': ['clear'], 'c': ['clear'], 'C': ['clear'] }; const numpadMap = { 'Numpad0': ['number', '0'], 'Numpad1': ['number', '1'], 'Numpad2': ['number', '2'], 'Numpad3': ['number', '3'], 'Numpad4': ['number', '4'], 'Numpad5': ['number', '5'], 'Numpad6': ['number', '6'], 'Numpad7': ['number', '7'], 'Numpad8': ['number', '8'], 'Numpad9': ['number', '9'], 'NumpadDecimal': ['decimal'], 'NumpadAdd': ['operator', '+'], 'NumpadSubtract': ['operator', '-'], 'NumpadMultiply': ['operator', '*'], 'NumpadDivide': ['operator', '/'], 'NumpadEnter': ['equals'], }; document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => { e.preventDefault(); const mapping = numpadMap[e.code] || keyMap[e.key]; if (mapping) { const [action, value] = mapping; if (actions[action]) { actions[action](value); updateDisplay(); const btn = document.querySelector(`[data-action="${action}"][data-value="${value || ''}"]`) || document.querySelector(`[data-action="${action}"]`); if (btn) buttonFeedback(btn); } } if (e.shiftKey && e.key === '(') { appendParen('('); updateDisplay(); } if (e.shiftKey && e.key === ')') { appendParen(')'); updateDisplay(); } }); Step 4: History Panel with localStorage Build a history panel that stores calculation results with timestamps and allows reusing previous results. const HISTORY_KEY = 'calc-history'; const MAX_HISTORY = 50; const History = { getAll() { try { return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(HISTORY_KEY)) || []; } catch { return []; } }, add(expression, result) { const history = this.getAll(); history.unshift({ id: Date.now(), expression, result: formatNumber(result), timestamp: new Date().toLocaleString() }); if (history.length > MAX_HISTORY) history.pop(); localStorage.setItem(HISTORY_KEY, JSON.stringify(history)); return history; }, clear() { localStorage.removeItem(HISTORY_KEY); }, delete(id) { const history = this.getAll().filter(h => h.id !== id); localStorage.setItem(HISTORY_KEY, JSON.stringify(history)); return history; } }; function renderHistory() { const panel = document.getElementById('history-panel'); const history = History.getAll(); if (history.length === 0) { panel.innerHTML = ' No calculations yet '; return; } panel.innerHTML = history.map(item => ` ${escapeHtml(item.expression)} = ${item.result} ${item.timestamp} `).join(''); panel.querySelectorAll('.history-reuse').forEach((btn, i) => { btn.addEventListener('click', () => { const result = history[i].result.replace(/,/g, ''); setExpression(result); closeHistory(); }); }); panel.querySelectorAll('.history-delete').forEach((btn, i) => { btn.addEventListener('click', () => { History.delete(history[i].id); renderHistory(); }); }); } Step 5: Expression Display with Formatting Build the display system that shows the expression with proper formatting and the current result in a separate line. Handle overflow with font-size reduction. const state = { expression: '', result: null, memory: 0, newExpression: true, justEvaluated: false }; function updateDisplay() { const exprEl = document.getElementById('calc-expression'); const resultEl = document.getElementById('calc-result'); exprEl.textContent = state.expression || '0'; if (state.result !== null) { resultEl.textContent = '= ' + formatNumber(state.result); resultEl.style.display = 'block'; } else { resultEl.style.display = 'none'; } adjustFontSize(exprEl); adjustFontSize(resultEl); } function adjustFontSize(el) { const maxWidth = el.parentElement.clientWidth - 40; let fontSize = 2; el.style.fontSize = fontSize + 'rem'; while (el.scrollWidth > maxWidth && fontSize > 0.8) { fontSize -= 0.1; el.style.fontSize = fontSize + 'rem'; } } function formatNumber(num) { if (!isFinite(num)) return 'Error'; if (Math.abs(num) >= 1e15 || (Math.abs(num)
sections.Testing Guide: Basic Operations: Test 2+2=4, 10-5=5, 3*7=21, 15/3=5. Test decimal operations: 0.1+0.2=0.3 (verify floating-point handling). Test negative numbers: -5+3=-2. Operator Precedence: Test 2+3*4=14 (not 20). Test (2+3)*4=20. Test 2^3^2=512 (right-associative). Test sqrt(16)=4, 5%=0.05. Error Handling: Test division by zero, invalid expressions (2++2), unmatched parentheses, overflow (999999^999999). Verify user-friendly error messages appear. Keyboard Testing: Test all number keys, operators, Enter, Escape, Backspace. Test numpad keys. Test shift+( and shift+). Verify keyboard doesn't interfere with other page interactions. History Testing: Perform calculations and verify they appear in history. Reuse a history item. Delete individual items. Clear all history. Verify history persists across page refreshes.
sections.Deployment Guide: This is a fully client-side application. Deploy to any static hosting: GitHub Pages (enable from main branch), Netlify (drag-drop or connect repo), Vercel (vercel CLI or import), or any web server. No build step is required - the calculator works out of the box. Ensure all paths are relative for subdirectory deployment.
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Using eval() for Expression Evaluation eval() executes arbitrary JavaScript code, making it a severe security risk. If a user types "2+alert('xss')", eval() will execute the alert. Always use a proper parser like the Shunting Yard algorithm. If you must use eval (e.g., for very complex math), sanitize the input rigorously with a whitelist regex: /^[\d+\-*/().%^,\s]+$/. 2. Floating-Point Precision Issues 0.1 + 0.2 in JavaScript equals 0.30000000000000004 due to IEEE 754 binary representation. Never compare floating-point numbers directly with ===. Use Number.EPSILON for comparisons. For display, use toFixed() or toPrecision() with appropriate rounding. For financial calculations, use integer arithmetic (cents instead of dollars). 3. Operator Precedence and Associativity Mistakes Many calculator implementations get operator precedence wrong, especially for exponentiation (which is right-associative: 2^3^2 = 512, not 64). Unary operators (negation) should have higher precedence than binary operators. Parentheses must be properly tracked with a counter to detect mismatches. Test with edge cases like consecutive operators, leading operators, and trailing operators. 4. Keyboard Event Conflicts with Browser Shortcuts Without proper handling, keyboard shortcuts like Ctrl+W (close tab), Ctrl+T (new tab), or browser back/forward (Alt+Left) can interfere with calculator input. Use e.preventDefault() to stop default browser behavior for handled keys. Avoid overriding system-level shortcuts. Check for modifier keys (ctrlKey, metaKey, altKey) before handling key events.
sections.Interview Questions: Q: Explain the Shunting Yard algorithm and its use cases beyond calculators. A: The Shunting Yard algorithm, developed by Edsger Dijkstra, converts infix notation (human-readable: "3 + 4") to postfix/RPN notation (machine-evaluable: "3 4 +"). It uses two stacks: one for operators and one for output. Operators are moved to the output stack based on precedence and associativity rules. Beyond calculators, it's used in: SQL query parsers, spreadsheet formula engines (Excel), mathematical typesetting (LaTeX), programming language compilers, and any system that needs to evaluate mathematical or logical expressions. Q: How do you handle floating-point precision in JavaScript? A: JavaScript uses IEEE 754 double-precision binary representation, causing precision issues with decimal fractions (0.1 + 0.2 !== 0.3). Strategies: use toFixed(n) for display rounding; multiply by a power of 10 to work with integers; use a library like decimal.js for arbitrary precision; use Number.EPSILON for approximate equality checks; for currency, always use integer cents. Never use === for floating-point comparison. Example: Math.abs(0.1 + 0.2 - 0.3) Q: What is event delegation and why would you use it for a calculator with 30+ buttons? A: Event delegation means attaching a single event listener to a parent element and using event.target to determine which child triggered it. For a calculator with 30+ buttons, this avoids 30+ separate event listeners, reducing memory usage and setup code. It automatically handles dynamically added buttons. The pattern: parent.addEventListener('click', (e) => { const btn = e.target.closest('.calc-btn'); if (!btn) return; handleAction(btn.dataset.action, btn.dataset.value); }). Q: How would you implement undo functionality for a calculator? A: Implement a state history stack that stores snapshots of the calculator state (expression, result, memory) after each action. The undo action pops the last state and restores it. Limit the stack to 50 entries to prevent memory issues. For a simpler approach, maintain an expression history array and a pointer to the current expression index, allowing both undo and redo by moving the pointer forward and backward through history. Q: Explain the difference between prefix, infix, and postfix notation. A: Infix: operator between operands (3 + 4), requires parentheses and precedence rules, used in everyday math. Prefix (Polish): operator before operands (+ 3 4), no parentheses needed, used in Lisp dialects. Postfix (Reverse Polish): operator after operands (3 4 +), no parentheses or precedence needed, easy to evaluate with a stack, used in old HP calculators and stack-based virtual machines. RPN evaluation is O(n) with a single pass using a stack, making it efficient for expression evaluation.
sections.Resume Description: Engineered a scientific-grade calculator web application with safe expression evaluation using the Shunting Yard algorithm, supporting unary/binary operators, functions, and constant parsing Implemented a tokenizer and RPN evaluator handling operator precedence, parenthetical grouping, and floating-point precision with Number.EPSILON-based comparisons Architected an event delegation system processing 30+ calculator actions through a single event listener, reducing memory footprint and enabling unified keyboard/mouse input handling Built a full keyboard support system with key mapping for standard keys, numpad, and modifier combinations, including browser shortcut conflict prevention Developed a persistent calculation history panel with localStorage CRUD operations, one-click result reuse, and timestamp tracking with a 50-entry limit Implemented CSS custom property theming with smooth transitions across all interactive elements and persisted user preference via localStorage Designed a memory management system (MC/MR/M+/M-) enabling multi-value storage and recall during complex calculation workflows
sections.GitHub Best Practices: README.md: Include screenshots of both themes, a live demo link, feature list with scientific functions, and a "Try it!" section with keyboard shortcut cheat sheet. Commit Messages: feat: implement Shunting Yard expression parser, fix: handle unary negation operator precedence, refactor: extract keyboard handler to separate module, test: add unit tests for parser edge cases. Testing: Write Jest unit tests for the parser module (tokenize, shuntingYard, evaluateRPN). Test with edge cases: empty input, division by zero, nested parentheses, unary minus, and large numbers. Use GitHub Actions to run tests on every PR.
sections.Extension Ideas: Scientific Mode: Add trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan with degree/radian toggle), logarithms (log, ln), factorial, and constants (PI, E, Euler's constant). Add a mode toggle button that expands the grid to show scientific buttons. Graphing Calculator: Add a canvas-based graph panel where users enter a function expression (e.g., "sin(x)") and see it plotted in real-time. Implement zoom, pan, and trace features. Use requestAnimationFrame for smooth rendering. Unit Converter: Add a unit conversion module alongside the calculator. Support common conversions: length (meters/feet), temperature (C/F/K), weight (kg/lbs), currency (with live exchange rate API), and data storage (MB/GB/TB). Voice Input: Integrate the Web Speech API for voice-controlled calculations. Users can say "two plus three times four" and see the expression and result. Add voice feedback that reads the result aloud.

description: Analytics dashboard displaying social media metrics with charts, filters, and data visualization.
title: Social Media Dashboard

sections.Project Overview: This social media analytics dashboard simulates a real-world analytics tool used by social media managers, marketers, and content creators to track performance across platforms. Users can view key metrics (followers, engagement, impressions, reach), visualize trends with multiple chart types (line, bar, doughnut, radar), filter data by date range, export reports as CSV/PNG, toggle between light and dark themes, and compare performance across different social platforms (Twitter, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube, TikTok). The project covers Chart.js integration, data normalization, responsive canvas management, and accessibility in data visualization.
sections.Problem Statement: Social media managers need to track KPIs across multiple platforms, identify trends, and generate reports - but native analytics tools are fragmented across platforms with different UIs, data formats, and export capabilities. Building a unified dashboard requires: aggregating mock or real data from multiple sources, rendering multiple chart types responsively, implementing date range filtering that updates all charts simultaneously, providing export functionality for reporting, and maintaining a clean UI that works in both light and dark environments. The challenge is creating a single-pane-of-glass dashboard that gives actionable insights at a glance.
sections.Real World Use Cases: Social Media Manager: A manager tracks weekly engagement rates across Twitter, Instagram, and LinkedIn, identifies top-performing content types, and exports monthly reports for stakeholders. Marketing Agency: An agency monitors client accounts across platforms, compares month-over-month growth, filters by campaign periods, and generates branded PDF reports. Content Creator: A YouTuber analyzes video performance, audience retention, subscriber growth trends, and determines optimal posting times using engagement heatmaps.
sections.Industry Relevance: Data visualization is a critical skill in modern web development. Chart.js is the most popular open-source charting library with over 65,000 GitHub stars, used by companies like Google, Apple, and Facebook. Dashboard architecture (KPIs + charts + filters) is the foundation for analytics products across every industry: marketing (Google Analytics, HubSpot), DevOps (Datadog, Grafana), finance (Bloomberg, Robinhood), and healthcare (Epic, Cerner). The ability to build responsive, accessible, and exportable data visualizations is a highly sought-after skill.
sections.Learning Outcomes: Integrate Chart.js with multiple chart types (Line, Bar, Doughnut, Radar, Polar Area) for diverse data representations Build a date range filtering system that reactively updates all dashboard charts and KPI cards simultaneously Implement data export to CSV for raw data and Canvas-to-PNG for chart screenshots using html2canvas Create responsive charts that resize correctly across viewport changes using Chart.js responsive + maintainAspectRatio options Implement dark/light theme toggle with CSS custom properties that dynamically update Chart.js grid and text colors Generate mock social media data with realistic patterns (growth trends, seasonal variations, random noise) Build KPI summary cards with trend indicators (up/down arrows, percentage change) and animated counter transitions Implement dashboard layout with CSS Grid for responsive widget arrangement and drag-and-drop widget reordering
sections.Technologies Used: Chart.js 4 JavaScript ES6+ localStorage html2canvas CSS Custom Properties HTML5 Canvas CSS Grid Font Awesome
sections.Prerequisites: Intermediate JavaScript: arrays, objects, map/reduce/filter, spread operator, destructuring DOM manipulation and event handling for interactive controls CSS Grid for layout and CSS custom properties for theming Basic understanding of Canvas API (helpful but not required) Familiarity with date manipulation (Date object, date-fns or similar)
sections.Architecture Overview: The dashboard follows a component-based architecture with a central data store, chart manager, filter system, and export service. The DataGenerator produces realistic mock data with configurable parameters (platform mix, growth rate, seasonality). The ChartManager is a singleton that creates, updates, and destroys Chart.js instances, handling responsive resize events and theme changes. The FilterController manages date range selections and broadcasts filter changes to all subscribers (charts + KPI cards + export). The ThemeManager toggles CSS custom properties and updates Chart.js default colors. The ExportService handles CSV generation from raw data and PNG capture from canvas elements using html2canvas. Widget positions are persisted in localStorage for drag-and-drop reordering. The dashboard is organized in a CSS Grid layout with defined template areas for KPI cards, charts, and data tables.
sections.Folder Structure: social-dashboard/ ├── index.html ├── css/ │ └── style.css ├── js/ │ ├── app.js │ ├── data.js # Mock data generator │ ├── charts.js # Chart.js manager │ ├── filters.js # Date range filtering │ ├── kpi.js # KPI card renderer │ ├── export.js # CSV + PNG export │ ├── theme.js # Dark/light theme toggle │ └── grid.js # Drag-and-drop widget grid ├── assets/ │ └── icons/ ├── .gitignore └── README.md
sections.Development Roadmap: Phase 1: Data Generation & KPI Cards Build the mock data generator that produces realistic social media metrics (followers, likes, comments, shares, impressions, reach, engagement rate) for multiple platforms over configurable time ranges. Create animated KPI cards showing current values, percentage changes, and trend arrows. Phase 2: Chart.js Integration Install Chart.js from CDN. Build the chart manager that creates and manages multiple chart instances. Implement: line chart for followers over time, bar chart for engagement metrics, doughnut chart for platform distribution, radar chart for cross-platform comparison, and area chart for impressions/reach trends. Phase 3: Date Range Filtering Build the date range filter with preset buttons (7D, 30D, 90D, 1Y, All) and custom date picker. Implement the filter event system that broadcasts date range changes to all charts, KPI cards, and the data table. All dashboard components update reactively when the filter changes. Phase 4: Export Functionality Implement CSV export that generates downloadable files from the current filtered data. Implement chart export as PNG images using the Canvas API (chartInstance.toBase64Image()). Build a combined report export option that captures the entire dashboard as a single PNG using html2canvas. Phase 5: Theme & Responsive Design Implement dark/light theme toggle with CSS custom properties. Update Chart.js configuration dynamically on theme change (grid lines, text colors, backgrounds). Make all charts responsive with resize handlers. Implement widget drag-and-drop using HTML5 DnD API with localStorage persistence.
sections.Implementation Guide: Step 1: Mock Data Generator with Realistic Patterns Generate realistic social media metrics with growth trends, weekly seasonality, and random noise. Data covers multiple platforms and metrics. const PLATFORMS = ['twitter', 'instagram', 'linkedin', 'youtube', 'tiktok']; const METRICS = ['followers', 'likes', 'comments', 'shares', 'impressions', 'reach', 'engagementRate']; function generateMockData(days = 365) { const data = []; const startDate = new Date(); startDate.setDate(startDate.getDate() - days); PLATFORMS.forEach(platform => { const growthRate = getGrowthRate(platform); const baseFollowers = getBaseFollowers(platform); for (let day = 0; day = 1 && day = 9 && month = 0 && month = 5 && month Step 2: Chart.js Manager with Multiple Chart Types Build a chart manager that creates, updates, and destroys Chart.js instances. Handles responsive resizing and theme changes. class ChartManager { constructor() { this.charts = new Map(); this.defaultColors = { twitter: '#1DA1F2', instagram: '#E4405F', linkedin: '#0A66C2', youtube: '#FF0000', tiktok: '#000000' }; } createLineChart(canvasId, labels, datasets, options = {}) { this.destroyChart(canvasId); const ctx = document.getElementById(canvasId)?.getContext('2d'); if (!ctx) return; const chart = new Chart(ctx, { type: 'line', data: { labels, datasets }, options: { responsive: true, maintainAspectRatio: false, plugins: { legend: { position: 'bottom', labels: { usePointStyle: true } }, tooltip: { mode: 'index', intersect: false } }, scales: { x: { grid: { display: false }, ticks: { maxTicksLimit: 10 } }, y: { beginAtZero: true, grid: { color: getComputedStyle(document.documentElement).getPropertyValue('--chart-grid-color') || 'rgba(0,0,0,0.05)' } } }, interaction: { mode: 'nearest', axis: 'x', intersect: false }, ...options } }); this.charts.set(canvasId, chart); return chart; } createDoughnutChart(canvasId, labels, data, colors) { this.destroyChart(canvasId); const ctx = document.getElementById(canvasId)?.getContext('2d'); if (!ctx) return; const chart = new Chart(ctx, { type: 'doughnut', data: { labels, datasets: [{ data, backgroundColor: colors || ['#1DA1F2', '#E4405F', '#0A66C2', '#FF0000', '#00f2ea'], borderWidth: 2, borderColor: '#fff' }] }, options: { responsive: true, maintainAspectRatio: false, plugins: { legend: { position: 'bottom', labels: { usePointStyle: true, padding: 15 } }, tooltip: { callbacks: { label: (ctx) => { const total = ctx.dataset.data.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0); const pct = ((ctx.parsed / total) * 100).toFixed(1); return `${ctx.label}: ${ctx.parsed.toLocaleString()} (${pct}%)`; } } } }, cutout: '65%', animation: { animateRotate: true } } }); this.charts.set(canvasId, chart); return chart; } createBarChart(canvasId, labels, datasets) { this.destroyChart(canvasId); const ctx = document.getElementById(canvasId)?.getContext('2d'); if (!ctx) return; const chart = new Chart(ctx, { type: 'bar', data: { labels, datasets }, options: { responsive: true, maintainAspectRatio: false, plugins: { legend: { position: 'bottom' } }, scales: { x: { grid: { display: false } }, y: { beginAtZero: true, grid: { color: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.05)' } } } } }); this.charts.set(canvasId, chart); return chart; } createRadarChart(canvasId, labels, datasets) { this.destroyChart(canvasId); const ctx = document.getElementById(canvasId)?.getContext('2d'); if (!ctx) return; const chart = new Chart(ctx, { type: 'radar', data: { labels, datasets }, options: { responsive: true, maintainAspectRatio: false, plugins: { legend: { position: 'bottom' } }, scales: { r: { beginAtZero: true, grid: { color: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.1)' }, pointLabels: { font: { size: 12 } } } } } }); this.charts.set(canvasId, chart); return chart; } updateChartColors(theme) { const gridColor = theme === 'dark' ? 'rgba(255,255,255,0.05)' : 'rgba(0,0,0,0.05)'; this.charts.forEach(chart => { chart.options.scales?.y?.grid && (chart.options.scales.y.grid.color = gridColor); chart.options.scales?.x?.grid && (chart.options.scales.x.grid.color = 'transparent'); chart.options.scales?.r?.grid && (chart.options.scales.r.grid.color = gridColor); chart.update('none'); }); } resizeAll() { this.charts.forEach(chart => chart.resize()); } destroyChart(id) { if (this.charts.has(id)) { this.charts.get(id).destroy(); this.charts.delete(id); } } destroyAll() { this.charts.forEach(chart => chart.destroy()); this.charts.clear(); } } const chartManager = new ChartManager(); Step 3: Date Range Filtering with Reactive Updates Build a filter system that allows presets and custom date ranges, broadcasting changes to all dashboard components via custom events. class FilterController { constructor() { this.dateRange = this.getDefaultRange(); this.callbacks = []; this.init(); } getDefaultRange() { const end = new Date(); const start = new Date(); start.setDate(start.getDate() - 30); return { start, end, preset: '30D' }; } init() { const presetBtns = document.querySelectorAll('.preset-btn'); presetBtns.forEach(btn => { btn.addEventListener('click', () => { presetBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active')); btn.classList.add('active'); this.setPreset(btn.dataset.range); }); }); const startInput = document.getElementById('date-start'); const endInput = document.getElementById('date-end'); startInput?.addEventListener('change', (e) => { this.dateRange.start = new Date(e.target.value); this.dateRange.preset = 'custom'; this.apply(); }); endInput?.addEventListener('change', (e) => { this.dateRange.end = new Date(e.target.value); this.dateRange.preset = 'custom'; this.apply(); }); } setPreset(preset) { const end = new Date(); const start = new Date(); switch (preset) { case '7D': start.setDate(start.getDate() - 7); break; case '30D': start.setDate(start.getDate() - 30); break; case '90D': start.setDate(start.getDate() - 90); break; case '1Y': start.setFullYear(start.getFullYear() - 1); break; case 'ALL': start.setFullYear(2020, 0, 1); break; default: start.setDate(start.getDate() - 30); } this.dateRange = { start, end, preset }; document.getElementById('date-start').value = start.toISOString().split('T')[0]; document.getElementById('date-end').value = end.toISOString().split('T')[0]; this.apply(); } filterData(data) { return data.filter(d => { const date = new Date(d.date); return date >= this.dateRange.start && date { this.callbacks = this.callbacks.filter(c => c !== callback); }; } apply() { const event = new CustomEvent('filter:changed', { detail: { ...this.dateRange } }); document.dispatchEvent(event); this.callbacks.forEach(cb => cb(this.dateRange)); } } const filters = new FilterController(); // KPI Card component that updates on filter change class KPICard { constructor(containerId, config) { this.container = document.getElementById(containerId); this.config = config; // { title, metric, platform, format } this.init(); } init() { filters.onChange((range) => this.update(range)); this.update(filters.dateRange); } update(range) { const filtered = filters.filterData(window.mockData); const current = this.calculateMetric(filtered, range); const previous = this.calculateMetric(filtered, this.getPreviousRange(range)); const change = previous > 0 ? ((current - previous) / previous * 100) : 0; this.render(current, change); } calculateMetric(data, range) { const filtered = data.filter(d => { const date = new Date(d.date); return date >= range.start && date d.platform === this.config.platform); const values = platformData.map(d => d[this.config.metric]); return values.length > 0 ? values.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / values.length : 0; } const values = filtered.map(d => d[this.config.metric]); return values.length > 0 ? values.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) : 0; } getPreviousRange(current) { const diff = current.end - current.start; return { start: new Date(current.start.getTime() - diff), end: new Date(current.start) }; } render(value, change) { const formatted = this.config.format === 'pct' ? value.toFixed(2) + '%' : value >= 1000000 ? (value / 1000000).toFixed(1) + 'M' : value >= 1000 ? (value / 1000).toFixed(1) + 'K' : Math.round(value).toLocaleString(); const up = change >= 0; this.container.innerHTML = ` ${this.config.title} ${formatted} ${Math.abs(change).toFixed(1)}% vs previous period `; } } Step 4: Data Export to CSV and PNG Implement export functionality for CSV (raw data) and PNG (chart images and full dashboard screenshots). class ExportService { exportCSV(data, filename = 'social-media-report.csv') { if (!data || data.length === 0) { showToast('No data to export', 'warning'); return; } const headers = Object.keys(data[0]); const csvRows = [ headers.join(','), ...data.map(row => headers.map(h => { const val = row[h]; return typeof val === 'string' && val.includes(',') ? `"${val}"` : val; }).join(',') ) ]; const csvContent = csvRows.join('\n'); const blob = new Blob([csvContent], { type: 'text/csv;charset=utf-8;' }); const link = document.createElement('a'); link.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob); link.download = filename; link.click(); URL.revokeObjectURL(link.href); showToast('CSV exported successfully', 'success'); } exportChartPNG(chartId, filename = 'chart.png') { const chart = chartManager.charts.get(chartId); if (!chart) { showToast('Chart not found', 'error'); return; } const link = document.createElement('a'); link.download = filename; link.href = chart.toBase64Image('image/png', 1); link.click(); showToast('Chart exported as PNG', 'success'); } async exportFullDashboardPNG(filename = 'dashboard-report.png') { showToast('Generating dashboard screenshot...', 'info'); try { const dashboard = document.getElementById('dashboard'); const canvas = await html2canvas(dashboard, { backgroundColor: getComputedStyle(document.documentElement) .getPropertyValue('--bg').trim() || '#ffffff', scale: 2, useCORS: true, logging: false }); const link = document.createElement('a'); link.download = filename; link.href = canvas.toDataURL('image/png'); link.click(); showToast('Dashboard exported as PNG', 'success'); } catch (error) { showToast('Failed to export dashboard: ' + error.message, 'error'); } } exportCombinedReport() { const data = filters.filterData(window.mockData); this.exportCSV(data); setTimeout(() => this.exportFullDashboardPNG(), 500); } } const exporter = new ExportService(); // Button handlers document.getElementById('export-csv').addEventListener('click', () => { const filtered = filters.filterData(window.mockData); exporter.exportCSV(filtered); }); document.getElementById('export-png').addEventListener('click', () => { exporter.exportFullDashboardPNG(); }); document.getElementById('export-chart').addEventListener('click', () => { exporter.exportChartPNG('followers-chart', 'followers-trend.png'); }); Step 5: Dark Mode Theme with Chart.js Synchronization Implement a theme toggle that switches between light and dark modes, updating both CSS custom properties and Chart.js configuration synchronously. class ThemeManager { constructor() { this.theme = localStorage.getItem('dashboard-theme') || 'light'; this.apply(this.theme); this.init(); } init() { const toggle = document.getElementById('theme-toggle'); toggle?.addEventListener('click', () => { this.toggle(); }); document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => { if (e.key === 't' && (e.ctrlKey || e.metaKey)) { e.preventDefault(); this.toggle(); } }); } toggle() { this.theme = this.theme === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light'; this.apply(this.theme); } apply(theme) { document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', theme); localStorage.setItem('dashboard-theme', theme); // Update Chart.js defaults const isDark = theme === 'dark'; Chart.defaults.color = isDark ? '#94a3b8' : '#64748b'; Chart.defaults.borderColor = isDark ? 'rgba(255,255,255,0.05)' : 'rgba(0,0,0,0.05)'; // Update grid colors on existing charts chartManager.updateChartColors(theme); // Update toggle icon const icon = document.getElementById('theme-toggle')?.querySelector('i'); if (icon) { icon.className = isDark ? 'fas fa-sun' : 'fas fa-moon'; } // Add transition class for smooth theme change document.body.classList.add('theme-transitioning'); clearTimeout(this.transitionTimer); this.transitionTimer = setTimeout(() => { document.body.classList.remove('theme-transitioning'); }, 300); } } const themeManager = new ThemeManager(); // Detect system preference const prefersDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)'); prefersDark.addEventListener('change', (e) => { if (!localStorage.getItem('dashboard-theme')) { themeManager.apply(e.matches ? 'dark' : 'light'); } }); // Initialize dashboard with all components document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', async () => { window.mockData = generateMockData(365); // Render KPI cards const kpiConfigs = [ { containerId: 'kpi-followers', title: 'Total Followers', metric: 'followers', icon: 'users' }, { containerId: 'kpi-engagement', title: 'Avg Engagement Rate', metric: 'engagementRate', format: 'pct', icon: 'chart-line' }, { containerId: 'kpi-impressions', title: 'Total Impressions', metric: 'impressions', icon: 'eye' }, { containerId: 'kpi-reach', title: 'Total Reach', metric: 'reach', icon: 'bullseye' } ]; kpiConfigs.forEach(cfg => new KPICard(cfg.containerId, cfg)); // Create charts function updateCharts(range) { const filtered = filters.filterData(window.mockData); // Aggregate by date const byDate = {}; filtered.forEach(d => { if (!byDate[d.date]) byDate[d.date] = {}; byDate[d.date][d.platform] = d; }); const dates = Object.keys(byDate).sort(); const labels = dates.map(d => { const date = new Date(d); return date.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric' }); }); // Followers line chart chartManager.createLineChart('followers-chart', labels, PLATFORMS.map(p => ({ label: p.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + p.slice(1), data: dates.map(d => byDate[d][p]?.followers || 0), borderColor: chartManager.defaultColors[p], backgroundColor: chartManager.defaultColors[p] + '20', fill: true, tension: 0.4 }))); // Platform doughnut chart const totals = PLATFORMS.map(p => { const platformData = filtered.filter(d => d.platform === p); return platformData.reduce((sum, d) => sum + d.followers, 0); }); chartManager.createDoughnutChart('platform-chart', PLATFORMS.map(p => p.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + p.slice(1)), totals, PLATFORMS.map(p => chartManager.defaultColors[p]) ); // Engagement bar chart const engagementByPlatform = PLATFORMS.map(p => { const platformData = filtered.filter(d => d.platform === p); const avg = platformData.reduce((sum, d) => sum + d.engagementRate, 0) / platformData.length; return +avg.toFixed(2); }); chartManager.createBarChart('engagement-chart', PLATFORMS.map(p => p.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + p.slice(1)), [{ label: 'Engagement Rate (%)', data: engagementByPlatform, backgroundColor: PLATFORMS.map(p => chartManager.defaultColors[p] + '80'), borderColor: PLATFORMS.map(p => chartManager.defaultColors[p]), borderWidth: 2, borderRadius: 6 }] ); } filters.onChange(updateCharts); updateCharts(filters.dateRange); });
sections.Testing Guide: Chart Testing: Verify all five chart types render correctly with data. Test responsive resizing by dragging the browser window. Change the date range and verify all charts update simultaneously. Toggle dark mode and verify chart colors update correctly. KPI Cards: Verify numbers display in correct format (1.2K, 3.5M, 4.56%). Check trend arrows and percentage changes are calculated correctly by comparing with manual calculations. Verify animated counter transitions work. Date Range Filtering: Test each preset button (7D, 30D, 90D, 1Y, All). Set a custom date range with the date pickers. Verify the active preset button is highlighted. Switch between presets rapidly and verify no race conditions. Export Testing: Click CSV export and verify the file downloads with correct data. Test chart PNG export and verify the image quality. Test full dashboard export with html2canvas and verify it captures all visible content. Test export with no data and verify the error message. Theme Testing: Toggle between light and dark mode. Verify all text, backgrounds, and chart elements update. Refresh the page and verify the theme persists. Verify the toggle icon (moon/sun) matches the current theme.
sections.Deployment Guide: This is a fully client-side application with no backend dependencies. Deploy to any static hosting: GitHub Pages (enable from main branch), Netlify (drag-drop or connect repo), Vercel (import project), or any web server. The app uses Chart.js CDN, html2canvas CDN, and Font Awesome CDN - ensure these are loaded. All data is generated client-side (mock data) and stored in localStorage. For production, connect to real API endpoints (Twitter API, Instagram Graph API, LinkedIn API) via serverless functions.
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Chart.js Canvas Memory Leaks Creating new Chart.js instances without destroying old ones causes canvas elements to accumulate in memory. Charts continue to run animation frames and event listeners even when off-screen. Always call chart.destroy() before creating a new chart on the same canvas element. Use a chart manager that tracks all instances and provides a centralized destroy method. On page navigation or theme change, destroy all charts before recreating them. 2. Responsive Chart Sizing Issues Charts that don't resize properly when the window changes or when a sidebar toggles. Always set responsive: true and maintainAspectRatio: false for fluid sizing. Charts need a parent container with explicit height (use CSS height or aspect-ratio). Without a defined height, charts collapse to 0px. Use ResizeObserver to detect container size changes and call chart.resize(). For dashboards with drag-and-drop widgets, charts need forced resize after reordering. 3. Incorrect Date Range Filtering Logic Date comparisons using JavaScript Date objects can be tricky - start and end boundaries inclusive/exclusive logic is often wrong. Always use start of day (setHours(0,0,0,0)) for start dates and end of day (setHours(23,59,59,999)) for end dates to capture full days. When calculating previous period comparisons, ensure the previous range has the same duration as the current range. Use date libraries (date-fns, dayjs) for reliable date arithmetic. 4. Theme Changes Not Reflecting on Existing Charts CSS custom properties change instantly but Chart.js doesn't automatically re-evaluate them. When switching themes, you must manually update Chart.js options (scales grid color, ticks color, legend labels color) and call chart.update('none') (with 'none' to prevent animation delay). Store the current theme's color values in CSS custom properties and read them with getComputedStyle when updating charts. Chart.defaults.color should also be updated for new chart instances.
sections.Interview Questions: Q: How does Chart.js handle canvas rendering and when should you use the 'none' update mode? A: Chart.js uses the HTML5 Canvas API for rendering. The chart.update() method re-renders the chart with new data/options. The mode parameter controls animation: 'default' animates transitions, 'none' skips animation (useful for rapid updates like theme changes or resize), 'reset' resets to initial state, and 'show' shows hidden datasets. Use 'none' for theme changes (no animation needed), resize events (prevent janky animations), and data updates that happen faster than the animation duration. Q: Explain the difference between Canvas and SVG for data visualization. When would you use each? A: Canvas uses pixel-based rendering on a bitmap grid. SVG uses vector-based XML elements. Canvas advantages: better performance for large datasets (10,000+ points), easier for pixel manipulation and animations. SVG advantages: sharp at any resolution, accessible (screen readers can read text elements), CSS-stylable, smaller file size for simple graphics, and supports event handling per element. For dashboards: use Canvas/Chart.js for complex charts with lots of data points, SVG/D3.js for simpler infographics, interactive maps, and charts where individual element interactivity is critical. Q: How do you make chart data accessible to screen readers? A: Canvas-based charts are inherently inaccessible. Strategies: (1) Provide an aria-label describing the chart type and summary of the data. (2) Include a data table below or alongside the chart with the same data (use role="table" for screen readers). (3) Use Chart.js built-in accessibility features: turn on accessibility plugin, provide descriptive labels, use patterns/hatching in addition to colors for data distinction. (4) For SVG charts, add role="img", aria-label, and title/desc elements. (5) Ensure color contrast meets WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for text on charts). Q: How would you implement real-time data updates on a dashboard (e.g., live social media metrics)? A: (1) Use WebSockets (Socket.io) for push-based updates from the server, listening for 'metrics:update' events. (2) On new data, push to the existing data array and remove old data to maintain a sliding window (e.g., last 24 hours). (3) Call chart.update('none') for instantaneous updates without animation delay. (4) Implement throttling (max 1 update per second) to prevent chart flickering during high-frequency updates. (5) Show a "Live" indicator with a pulsing dot. (6) For KPI cards, use requestAnimationFrame for smooth number transitions. (7) Pause updates when the tab is hidden (Page Visibility API) to save resources. Q: How do you handle large datasets in Chart.js without performance degradation? A: (1) Data decimation: reduce the number of data points by sampling (e.g., take every 10th point for 10,000+ datasets). (2) Use the Chart.js decimation plugin which automatically reduces data shown based on chart width. (3) Disable animations and transitions for large datasets. (4) Use stepped line charts instead of smooth (tension: 0.4) which requires more rendering calculations. (5) Enable the dataset's segment property to skip drawing invisible segments. (6) For real-time data, implement a sliding window of the last N points. (7) Consider using WebGL-based charting (Chart.js with WebGL renderer or deck.gl) for millions of data points.
sections.Resume Description: Architected a multi-platform social media analytics dashboard using Chart.js with 5+ chart types (line, bar, doughnut, radar, polar) handling 10,000+ data points with responsive resizing and dark mode support Implemented a reactive date range filtering system with preset buttons and custom date pickers that synchronously updates all charts, KPI cards, and data tables via a custom event-driven architecture Built a Chart.js manager singleton with lifecycle management (create/update/destroy/resize) preventing canvas memory leaks across theme changes and data updates Developed export functionality for CSV data download and PNG image capture of individual charts and full dashboard using html2canvas with high-DPI scaling Created a mock data generator producing realistic social media metrics with growth trends, weekday seasonality, and platform-specific patterns for five major platforms Implemented dark/light theme toggle with CSS custom properties synchronized with Chart.js configuration (grid colors, text colors, legend styles) and prefers-color-scheme OS-level detection Designed KPI summary cards with animated counter transitions, percentage change indicators, and trend arrows that update reactively on filter changes
sections.GitHub Best Practices: README.md: Include dashboard screenshots in both themes, feature highlights with icons, live demo link, and a "Quick Start" section. Add a mock data documentation explaining the data generation patterns and how to replace with real API data. Commit Messages: feat: implement Chart.js line chart for follower trends, feat: add CSV and PNG export functionality, fix: prevent chart memory leaks on theme change, perf: optimize chart rendering with 'none' update mode on resize, refactor: extract chart manager into singleton module. Code Quality: Use ESLint with the airbnb config. Document Chart.js configuration with JSDoc comments explaining the visual design decisions (why specific colors, tensions, and scales were chosen).
sections.Extension Ideas: Real API Integration: Replace mock data with real API calls to Twitter API (v2), Instagram Graph API, LinkedIn API, YouTube Data API, and TikTok API. Implement OAuth 2.0 authentication flow. Add rate limiting awareness and automatic token refresh. AI-Powered Insights: Integrate with OpenAI or Anthropic APIs to generate natural language insights from the data (e.g., "Your Instagram engagement increased 15% this week, driven by Reels content. Best posting time is 6-8 PM on weekdays."). Display insights as a scrolling ticker or cards. Scheduled Reports: Add a feature to schedule automated email reports. Users configure frequency (weekly, monthly) and recipients. Generate PDF reports using Puppeteer on the server. Send via SendGrid or AWS SES. Include performance benchmarks and competitor analysis. Widget Builder: Create a drag-and-drop widget builder where users can customize their dashboard layout, choose which metrics to display, configure chart types, and set custom thresholds for alerts. Persist layouts per user in IndexedDB or sync to a backend.
