description: A Google Docs-style collaborative editor with live cursors, version history, and conflict resolution.
title: Real-Time Collaboration Tool

sections.Project Overview: Build a real-time collaborative editing platform similar to Google Docs or Notion, supporting simultaneous multi-user editing with conflict resolution, presence awareness, version history, and rich text formatting. This project implements both Operational Transform (OT) and Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDT) for synchronization, WebSocket-based real-time communication, and a robust undo/redo system. The backend is built with Node.js + TypeScript using a custom WebSocket server (or Socket.io), Redis for pub/sub and state management, and PostgreSQL for document persistence. The frontend uses React with ProseMirror (or Slate.js) for rich text editing, and Canvas-based cursor rendering for multi-user presence. You will implement a custom sync engine, differential sync algorithm, and a scalable WebSocket room architecture supporting thousands of concurrent editors per document.
sections.Problem Statement: Real-time collaboration introduces fundamental distributed systems challenges: (1) Multiple users editing the same document simultaneously creates conflicts that must be resolved without data loss. (2) Network latency (50-300ms) means users see stale states and make edits on diverged versions. (3) Cursor positions, selections, and scroll positions must be synchronized across users. (4) Undo/redo becomes exponentially complex — should Alice undoing affect Bob's edits? (5) Scaling from 2 to 1000 editors per document requires fundamentally different architectures. Existing solutions (Google Docs, Notion, Coda) are proprietary or rely on Google's OT implementation. The challenge is to build a production-grade collaboration engine that handles all these concerns with sub-100ms operation propagation, offline editing support, and conflict resolution that never loses user data.
sections.Real World Use Cases: Collaborative Document Editing Team writes proposals, reports, and documentation together. Features: comments, suggestions mode, version history with branching, and approval workflows. Used by product and engineering teams. Pair Programming IDE Two or more developers edit code simultaneously. Language-specific features: AST-aware diffing, syntax highlighting synchronization, terminal sharing, and language server protocol integration via WebSocket. Whiteboard & Diagramming Real-time collaborative whiteboard with sticky notes, flowcharts, wireframes, and mind maps. Uses CRDT for canvas object synchronization with conflict resolution for overlapping edits. Project Management Board Kanban boards with real-time card updates, drag-and-drop synchronization across users, and live notifications. Each card position is a CRDT element in a 2D space.
sections.Industry Relevance: The real-time collaboration tools market is valued at $45B+ with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Notion leading. Figma's $20B valuation proved that real-time collaboration is essential for modern software. Key trends: (1) Local-first software — CRDT enables offline editing with sync-on-reconnect (Linear, Obsidian), (2) AI-powered collaboration — real-time suggestions and auto-completion with awareness of other users' edits, (3) Decentralized collaboration — peer-to-peer sync without central servers using CRDT, (4) Collaborative code editing — VS Code Live Share, Replit Multiplayer, (5) Embedded collaboration — adding real-time editing to any web app. This project directly builds skills for roles at Figma, Google Docs, Notion, Microsoft 365, and Coda.
sections.Learning Outcomes: Operational Transform (OT): Deep understanding of OT algorithms — transform operations against each other to maintain consistency. Implement client-server OT with acknowledgement (OT-with-server-control) and understand why OT is hard to scale to multiple users. CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types): Master CRDT theory and implementation — specifically RGA (Replicated Growable Array) for ordered sequences and LWW-Register for last-writer-wins semantics. Understand how CRDT achieves eventual consistency without a central server. WebSocket Architecture: Design scalable WebSocket infrastructure with rooms, namespaces, sticky sessions, and Redis pub/sub for horizontal scaling. Handle backpressure, reconnection, and message ordering. Presence Systems: Implement real-time presence — cursors (position, color, name), selections (ranges), online/offline status, and typing indicators. Use Canvas overlay for cursor rendering over the editing surface. Version History & Diffing: Build a version tree with branching (like Git for documents). Implement efficient diff algorithms (Myers' diff, patience diff) and snapshot compression. Conflict Resolution: Design conflict resolution strategies for different data types: text (OT/CRDT), rich text (formatting + text), objects (field-level merge), and lists (position-based merge). Scalability: Design a system supporting 1000+ concurrent editors per document using operation batching, incremental snapshots, and connection pooling.
sections.Technologies Used: Node.js 20 20 LTS Backend sync server Socket.io 4.7 WebSocket + fallback transport React 18 18.2 Frontend framework ProseMirror / Slate.js 6.3 Rich text editor PostgreSQL 16 Document persistence Redis 7.2 Pub/sub + ephemeral state CRDT (yjs/automerge) 13.6 Conflict resolution diff-match-patch 1.1 Diff algorithm Docker 24.0 Containerized deployment Prometheus 2.50 Metrics + monitoring
sections.Prerequisites: TypeScript Mastery: Strong TypeScript skills including generics, union types, discriminated unions, and advanced patterns. The sync engine uses complex generics to type operations across different data types. Node.js & Event-Driven Programming: Deep understanding of Node.js event loop, streams, EventEmitter patterns, and async error handling. The sync server handles thousands of concurrent operations per second. React & Immutable State: Experience with immutable state patterns (Immer.js or Immutable.js). Understanding of React's reconciliation and rendering optimization for real-time updates (100+ updates/second). Data Structures: Strong fundamentals in trees (RGA uses a linked-list tree), arrays (operation transforms), and graphs (version history DAG). Understanding of vector clocks and Lamport timestamps. Database & Caching: Experience with PostgreSQL and Redis. Understanding of transactional integrity for document saves and cache invalidation patterns for real-time data. Networking: Understanding of WebSocket protocol, HTTP/2, TCP vs UDP tradeoffs, and latency/bandwidth constraints for real-time systems.
sections.Architecture Overview: +------------------------------------------------------------------+ | COLLABORATION ENGINE ARCHITECTURE | +------------------------------------------------------------------+ | | | CLIENT A (React) CLIENT B (React) CLIENT C (React) | | +------------------+ +------------------+ +--------------+ | | | ProseMirror | | ProseMirror | | ProseMirror | | | | Editor | | Editor | | Editor | | | | + Sync Engine | | + Sync Engine | | + Sync Engine| | | | + Presence | | + Presence | | + Presence | | | | + OT Client | | + OT Client | | + OT Client | | | +--------+---------+ +--------+---------+ +------+-------+ | | | | | | | | WebSocket (wss) | WebSocket | | | +-------------------------+----------------------+ | | | | | +----------------------------------------------------------------+| | | SYNC SERVER (Node.js + Socket.io) || | | +--------------+ +--------------+ +----------------------+ || | | | OT Transform | | CRDT Merge | | Operation Log | || | | | Engine | | Engine | | (Append-only, Redis) | || | | +--------------+ +--------------+ +----------------------+ || | | +--------------+ +--------------+ +----------------------+ || | | | Presence | | Auth + Auth | | Document Manager | || | | | Hub | | Middleware | | (Version tree, snap) | || | | +--------------+ +--------------+ +----------------------+ || | | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ || | | | Redis Pub/Sub (cross-instance message relay) | || | | +-----------------------------------------------------------+ || | +----------------------------------------------------------------+| | | | | +----------------------------------------------------------------+| | | DATA LAYER | | | | +------------------+ +------------------+ +--------------+ || | | | PostgreSQL | | Redis Cache | | S3/Blob | || | | | - Documents | | - Current state | | Storage | || | | | - Versions | | - Active edits | | (Images, | || | | | - Users | | - Session data | | attachments)| || | | | - Comments | | - Pub/sub queues | | | || | | +------------------+ +------------------+ +--------------+ || | +----------------------------------------------------------------+| +------------------------------------------------------------------+ Data Flow: (1) User types → ProseMirror captures local operation. (2) Operation sent to sync engine which applies OT transformation against pending concurrent operations. (3) Transformed operation sent via WebSocket to server. (4) Server receives operation, applies server-side OT, broadcasts to all other clients. (5) Other clients receive operation, transform against their pending local operations, and apply to their ProseMirror document. (6) Presence data (cursor position) is sent separately at lower priority (every 50ms vs operations which are sent immediately).
sections.Database Design: Table Columns Description documents id (UUID PK), title, owner_id, team_id, created_at, updated_at, last_edited_by, version (INT), snapshot (JSONB), checksum Document metadata with current state snapshot document_versions id (BIGSERIAL PK), document_id (FK), version_number, parent_version, operations (JSONB[]), snapshot (JSONB), author_id, created_at, checksum Version tree with operation log and full snapshot at each version operations id (BIGSERIAL PK), document_id, version, client_id, operation_type (insert/delete/format), position, length, text, attributes (JSONB), server_timestamp, client_timestamp Append-only log of all operations for audit and replay comments id (UUID PK), document_id, anchor_position (INT), anchor_length (INT), text, author_id, resolved (BOOL), parent_comment_id, created_at, updated_at Document comments anchored to specific text ranges sessions id (UUID PK), document_id, user_id, joined_at, last_activity, cursor_position (JSONB), selection (JSONB), is_active (BOOL) Active editing sessions for presence tracking users id (UUID PK), email, name, avatar_url, color (for cursor display), created_at User accounts teams id (UUID PK), name, owner_id, created_at, plan_type Team/organization for document sharing document_permissions id (UUID PK), document_id, user_id/team_id, permission_level (view/edit/comment/admin), granted_by, created_at Access control with granular permissions

sections.UI/UX Design Strategy: The collaboration tool's design focuses on making multiuser editing feel natural and uncluttered: Cursor Presence: Each user gets a uniquely colored cursor with their name label. Cursors animate smoothly between positions using CSS transforms. Selection ranges shown as translucent colored highlights behind text. Cursor position updates at 20fps to balance bandwidth and smoothness. Minimal Interference: Remote cursors are visible but non-interactive — they don't interfere with clicking, selecting, or typing. Cursors fade out after 5 seconds of inactivity. User list in the header shows active editors with colored dots. Typing Indicators: When a user is typing, a small bubble appears in the margin showing their name and a typing animation. Prevents accidental interruption of someone who is actively typing. Conflict Awareness: When a conflict occurs that can't be automatically resolved, affected text is highlighted with a yellow background and a merge tool UI appears. Users can choose "accept mine", "accept theirs", or "view diff". Version History Timeline: A sidebar timeline shows all versions with author avatars, timestamps, and a visual diff preview. Users can click any version to see a side-by-side diff. Version names are auto-generated "Alice edited paragraph 3". Offline Indicator: When connection drops, a subtle banner appears: "Working offline — changes will sync when reconnected." Offline edits are queued and applied with operational transform when reconnecting.
sections.Development Roadmap: Phase 1: Single-User Editor (Week 1-2) Set up project scaffold with Vite + React + TypeScript. Integrate ProseMirror with basic text editing. Implement rich text formatting (bold, italic, headings, lists, links). Build toolbar UI with formatting buttons. Save/load documents from PostgreSQL. Phase 2: WebSocket Infrastructure (Week 3-4) Set up Node.js + Socket.io server. Implement WebSocket connection with authentication. Create DocumentRoom abstraction — each document gets a Socket.io room. Build heartbeat/ping-pong for connection health. Implement reconnection logic with exponential backoff. Phase 3: Operational Transform (Week 5-7) Implement OT transform functions for insert/delete/format operations. Build client-side pending operation buffer. Implement server-side OT with acknowledgement. Handle edge cases: concurrent insert at same position, concurrent delete of overlapping ranges. Write comprehensive tests for transform properties (TP1, TP2). Phase 4: Presence System (Week 8-9) Implement cursor position tracking and broadcasting. Build Canvas overlay for rendering remote cursors. Add selection highlighting with user colors. Implement typing indicators. Optimize: throttle cursor updates to 20fps, batch presence events. Phase 5: CRDT Integration (Week 10-12) Implement RGA (Replicated Growable Array) for the document text. Implement LWW-Register for document metadata. Replace OT with CRDT for text operations. Implement CRDT merge on reconnection. Handle offline editing with CRDT's natural merge properties. Benchmark OT vs CRDT performance. Phase 6: Version History (Week 13-14) Implement version snapshots (every 50 operations or 5 minutes). Build version tree with parent pointers. Implement diff computation between versions (Myers diff). Create version history UI with timeline, diffs, and restore functionality. Implement retention policy: keep every 10th version after 1000 versions. Phase 7: Comments & Collaboration Features (Week 15-16) Implement anchored comments (comment attached to specific text range, moves with edits). Add suggestion mode (tracked changes). Implement real-time comment notifications. Build sharing dialog with permission levels (view/comment/edit). Add activity log feed. Phase 8: Scaling & Production Readiness (Week 17-18) Implement Redis pub/sub for cross-instance message relay. Set up Socket.io Redis adapter. Load test with 1000 concurrent connections. Optimize memory usage (document snapshots, operation buffers). Implement rate limiting and connection limits. Add monitoring dashboards.
sections.Implementation Guide: Step 1: OT Core — Operation Types & Transform // server/src/sync/operation.ts export enum OperationType { Insert = 'insert', Delete = 'delete', Retain = 'retain', Format = 'format', } export interface InsertOp { type: OperationType.Insert; position: number; text: string; attributes?: Record ; clientId: string; seq: number; } export interface DeleteOp { type: OperationType.Delete; position: number; length: number; clientId: string; seq: number; } export interface FormatOp { type: OperationType.Format; position: number; length: number; attributes: Record ; clientId: string; seq: number; } export type Operation = InsertOp | DeleteOp | FormatOp; export interface OperationBatch { documentId: string; operations: Operation[]; baseVersion: number; clientId: string; } // server/src/sync/transform.ts import { Operation, OperationType } from './operation'; /** * OT Function f(op1, op2) -> [op1', op2'] * Transforms op1 against op2 so they can be applied in either order */ export function transform(op1: Operation, op2: Operation): [Operation, Operation] { // TP1: If ops are from the same client, they're already ordered if (op1.clientId === op2.clientId) { return [op1, op2]; } // Insert vs Insert if (op1.type === OperationType.Insert && op2.type === OperationType.Insert) { return transformInsertInsert(op1, op2); } // Delete vs Delete if (op1.type === OperationType.Delete && op2.type === OperationType.Delete) { return transformDeleteDelete(op1, op2); } // Insert vs Delete if (op1.type === OperationType.Insert && op2.type === OperationType.Delete) { return transformInsertDelete(op1, op2); } if (op1.type === OperationType.Delete && op2.type === OperationType.Insert) { const [op2Prime, op1Prime] = transformInsertDelete(op2, op1); return [op1Prime, op2Prime]; } // For format operations, treat like retains return transformRetainRetain(op1, op2); } function transformInsertInsert(op1: InsertOp, op2: InsertOp): [InsertOp, InsertOp] { // If at same position, use clientId as tiebreaker if (op1.position === op2.position) { if (op1.clientId = op2.position + op2.length) { // Insert after delete, insert shifts left return [ { ...op1, position: op1.position - op2.length }, op2, ]; } else { // Insert inside delete range — tricky // The insert survives (we keep it), but the delete splits around it // We split the delete into two: before insert and after insert // For simplicity, we keep the delete for the part before the insert const beforeLen = op1.position - op2.position; return [ { ...op1, position: op2.position + beforeLen }, { ...op2, length: beforeLen }, ]; } } /** * Compose: apply op1 then op2 = compose(op1, op2) */ export function compose(a: Operation, b: Operation): Operation { // Simplified compose — in production, handle all combinations if (a.type === OperationType.Insert && b.type === OperationType.Delete) { if (b.position >= a.position && b.position + b.length Step 2: CRDT — Replicated Growable Array (RGA) // server/src/sync/crdt/RGA.ts import { v7 as uuidv7 } from 'uuid'; interface RGANode { id: string; // Unique ID (UUID v7 for time-ordered) value: string | null; // null = tombstone (deleted) createdAt: number; // Lamport timestamp or hybrid logical clock creator: string; // Client ID that created this node next: string | null; // ID of next node in sequence } /** * RGA (Replicated Growable Array) * A CRDT for ordered sequences with eventual consistency. * Each character/operation is a node in a linked list. * Conflicts resolved by: insert position (parent), then creator ID, then timestamp. */ export class RGA { private nodes: Map = new Map(); private head: string | null = null; // ID of first node private clock: number = 0; constructor() { // Initialize with empty document head // In practice, the document is a sequence of RGA nodes } /** * Local insert: create a new node at the given position after the target node */ localInsert(afterId: string | null, value: string, creator: string): RGANode { this.clock++; const node: RGANode = { id: uuidv7(), value, createdAt: this.clock, creator, next: null, }; if (afterId === null) { // Insert at beginning node.next = this.head; this.head = node.id; } else { const afterNode = this.nodes.get(afterId); if (!afterNode) throw new Error(`Node ${afterId} not found`); node.next = afterNode.next; afterNode.next = node.id; } this.nodes.set(node.id, node); return node; } /** * Remote insert: merge an insert operation from another client * Conflict resolution: find the right position based on causal ordering */ remoteInsert(node: RGANode): void { this.clock = Math.max(this.clock, node.createdAt) + 1; if (!this.nodes.has(node.id)) { this.nodes.set(node.id, node); this.mergeInsert(node); } } private mergeInsert(newNode: RGANode): void { // Find the correct position by finding where this node would have been inserted // We need to walk from head and find the right spot based on causal history if (this.head === null) { this.head = newNode.id; newNode.next = null; return; } // If this node was inserted at position 0 (no predecessor) // Try to determine position from causal relationships let prev: string | null = null; let current = this.head; while (current !== null) { const currentNode = this.nodes.get(current)!; // Check if new node should go before current node if (this.shouldPrecede(newNode, currentNode)) { if (prev === null) { newNode.next = this.head; this.head = newNode.id; } else { const prevNode = this.nodes.get(prev)!; newNode.next = prevNode.next; prevNode.next = newNode.id; } return; } prev = current; current = currentNode.next; } // Append to end const lastNode = this.nodes.get(prev!)!; lastNode.next = newNode.id; newNode.next = null; } /** * Determine if node a should come before node b * Rules: (1) If a is a descendant of b's predecessor, a goes after b * (2) Otherwise, compare by creator ID, then timestamp */ private shouldPrecede(a: RGANode, b: RGANode): boolean { if (a.creator b.creator) return false; return a.createdAt ({ id, value: node.value, createdAt: node.createdAt, creator: node.creator, next: node.next, })), clock: this.clock, }; } static fromJSON(data: any): RGA { const rga = new RGA(); rga.head = data.head; rga.clock = data.clock; for (const nodeData of data.nodes) { rga.nodes.set(nodeData.id, { id: nodeData.id, value: nodeData.value, createdAt: nodeData.createdAt, creator: nodeData.creator, next: nodeData.next, }); } return rga; } } Step 3: Sync Engine — Client-Side OT // client/src/sync/OTClient.ts import { Socket } from 'socket.io-client'; import { Operation, OperationType, InsertOp, DeleteOp, FormatOp, OperationBatch } from '../../../shared/types'; import { transform, compose } from './transform'; type State = 'synchronized' | 'awaiting_ack' | 'awaiting_ack_with_buffer'; export class OTClient { private socket: Socket; private docId: string; private state: State = 'synchronized'; private pendingOp: Operation | null = null; private buffer: Operation[] = []; private version: number = 0; private onApplyRemote: (op: Operation) => void; private onAcknowledge: (op: Operation) => void; constructor( socket: Socket, docId: string, onApplyRemote: (op: Operation) => void, onAcknowledge: (op: Operation) => void ) { this.socket = socket; this.docId = docId; this.onApplyRemote = onApplyRemote; this.onAcknowledge = onAcknowledge; this.socket.on('doc:operation', this.handleRemoteOperation); this.socket.on('doc:ack', this.handleAck); } /** * Send a local operation to the server */ sendOperation(op: Operation): void { switch (this.state) { case 'synchronized': this.state = 'awaiting_ack'; this.pendingOp = op; this.socket.emit('doc:operation', { documentId: this.docId, operation: op, baseVersion: this.version, }); break; case 'awaiting_ack': this.state = 'awaiting_ack_with_buffer'; // Buffer new operation, but first transform it against pending const [transformedOp, newPending] = transform(op, this.pendingOp!); this.pendingOp = newPending; this.buffer.push(transformedOp); break; case 'awaiting_ack_with_buffer': // Transform against pending, then against all buffered let opToBuffer = op; for (const buffered of this.buffer) { const [tOp, _] = transform(opToBuffer, buffered); opToBuffer = tOp; } this.buffer.push(opToBuffer); break; } } /** * Handle operation received from server (broadcast from another client) */ private handleRemoteOperation = (data: { operation: Operation; version: number }) => { const { operation: remoteOp } = data; switch (this.state) { case 'synchronized': // Apply remote directly this.onApplyRemote(remoteOp); this.version++; break; case 'awaiting_ack': // Transform pending operation against remote, apply remote first const [pendingPrime, remotePrime] = transform(this.pendingOp!, remoteOp); this.pendingOp = pendingPrime; this.onApplyRemote(remotePrime); this.version++; break; case 'awaiting_ack_with_buffer': // Transform all pending ops against remote const [pendingPrime2, remotePrime2] = transform(this.pendingOp!, remoteOp); this.pendingOp = pendingPrime2; const newBuffer: Operation[] = []; for (const buffered of this.buffer) { const [bufferedPrime, _] = transform(buffered, remotePrime2); newBuffer.push(bufferedPrime); } this.buffer = newBuffer; this.onApplyRemote(remotePrime2); this.version++; break; } }; /** * Handle acknowledgement from server (our operation was committed) */ private handleAck = () => { switch (this.state) { case 'synchronized': // Shouldn't happen break; case 'awaiting_ack': this.onAcknowledge(this.pendingOp!); this.pendingOp = null; this.state = 'synchronized'; this.version++; break; case 'awaiting_ack_with_buffer': this.onAcknowledge(this.pendingOp!); // Send oldest buffered operation const nextOp = this.buffer.shift()!; this.pendingOp = nextOp; this.socket.emit('doc:operation', { documentId: this.docId, operation: nextOp, baseVersion: this.version, }); this.version++; if (this.buffer.length === 0) { this.state = 'awaiting_ack'; } break; } }; /** * Clean up on unmount */ disconnect(): void { this.socket.off('doc:operation', this.handleRemoteOperation); this.socket.off('doc:ack', this.handleAck); } } Step 4: WebSocket Document Room // server/src/websocket/DocumentRoom.ts import { Socket, Server as SocketIOServer } from 'socket.io'; import { Redis } from 'ioredis'; import { SyncServer } from '../sync/SyncServer'; import { PresenceManager } from '../presence/PresenceManager'; import { DocumentService } from '../api/services/DocumentService'; import { logger } from '../utils/logger'; export class DocumentRoom { private io: SocketIOServer; private syncServer: SyncServer; private presenceManager: PresenceManager; private documentService: DocumentService; private redis: Redis; private activeSockets: Map > = new Map(); // docId -> Set constructor( io: SocketIOServer, syncServer: SyncServer, presenceManager: PresenceManager, documentService: DocumentService, redis: Redis ) { this.io = io; this.syncServer = syncServer; this.presenceManager = presenceManager; this.documentService = documentService; this.redis = redis; } /** * Handle client joining a document room */ async handleJoin(socket: Socket, docId: string, userId: string) { try { // Verify user has access to document const hasAccess = await this.documentService.checkPermission(docId, userId, 'edit'); if (!hasAccess) { socket.emit('error', { code: 'FORBIDDEN', message: 'No access to this document' }); return; } // Get current document state const docState = await this.syncServer.getCurrentState(docId); // Join Socket.io room socket.join(`doc:${docId}`); // Track active sockets if (!this.activeSockets.has(docId)) { this.activeSockets.set(docId, new Set()); } this.activeSockets.get(docId)!.add(socket.id); // Send current state to client socket.emit('doc:state', { documentId: docId, version: docState.version, content: docState.content, operations: docState.recentOperations, }); // Register presence await this.presenceManager.userJoined(docId, userId, socket.id); // Broadcast user joined to room socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:join', { userId, user: await this.documentService.getUserInfo(userId), }); // Send active users to joining client const activeUsers = await this.presenceManager.getActiveUsers(docId); socket.emit('presence:active', activeUsers); logger.info(`User ${userId} joined document ${docId}`); // Set up event handlers for this socket this.setupSocketHandlers(socket, docId, userId); } catch (error) { logger.error(`Join error for doc ${docId}:`, error); socket.emit('error', { code: 'INTERNAL', message: 'Failed to join document' }); } } private setupSocketHandlers(socket: Socket, docId: string, userId: string) { // Handle operations from client socket.on('doc:operation', async (data: { operation: any; baseVersion: number }) => { try { const result = await this.syncServer.handleOperation( docId, userId, data.operation, data.baseVersion ); // Broadcast to other clients in room (with transformation applied) socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('doc:operation', { operation: result.transformedOp, version: result.newVersion, userId, }); // Acknowledge to sender socket.emit('doc:ack', { version: result.newVersion, serverTimestamp: result.timestamp, }); // Persist operation await this.documentService.persistOperation(docId, result); } catch (error) { logger.error(`Operation error:`, error); socket.emit('error', { code: 'OP_FAILED', message: 'Operation rejected' }); } }); // Handle presence updates socket.on('presence:update', (data: { cursor?: any; selection?: any }) => { this.presenceManager.updatePresence(docId, userId, { cursor: data.cursor, selection: data.selection, lastActivity: Date.now(), }); socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:update', { userId, cursor: data.cursor, selection: data.selection, }); }); // Handle cursor updates (high frequency, separate throttling) socket.on('presence:cursor', (data: { position: any }) => { socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:cursor', { userId, position: data.position, }); }); // Handle typing indicator socket.on('presence:typing', (isTyping: boolean) => { socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:typing', { userId, isTyping, }); }); // Handle disconnect socket.on('disconnect', async () => { await this.handleDisconnect(socket, docId, userId); }); } private async handleDisconnect(socket: Socket, docId: string, userId: string) { const sockets = this.activeSockets.get(docId); if (sockets) { sockets.delete(socket.id); // Check if user has any other sockets connected to this doc const userSockets = Array.from(sockets).filter(sid => { // In production, track userId per socket return true; }); if (userSockets.length === 0) { await this.presenceManager.userLeft(docId, userId); this.io.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:leave', { userId, }); } } } /** * Scale: broadcast to other instances via Redis pub/sub */ private async broadcastToOtherInstances(docId: string, event: string, data: any) { await this.redis.publish(`collab:${docId}`, JSON.stringify({ event, data, instanceId: process.env.INSTANCE_ID || 'default', })); } } Step 5: Cursor Overlay // client/src/editor/cursors.ts import { EditorView } from 'prosemirror-view'; import { UserPresence } from '../presence/PresenceClient'; interface RemoteCursor { userId: string; name: string; color: string; position: { from: number; to: number }; lastUpdate: number; } export class CursorOverlay { private view: EditorView; private cursors: Map = new Map(); private overlayElement: HTMLElement; private animationFrame: number | null = null; private userColors: string[] = [ '#6366f1', '#ef4444', '#22c55e', '#f59e0b', '#3b82f6', '#ec4899', '#14b8a6', '#f97316', ]; private colorIndex: number = 0; constructor(view: EditorView) { this.view = view; this.overlayElement = this.createOverlay(); document.body.appendChild(this.overlayElement); } private createOverlay(): HTMLElement { const overlay = document.createElement('div'); overlay.className = 'remote-cursors-overlay'; overlay.style.cssText = ` position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0; pointer-events: none; z-index: 100; overflow: hidden; `; return overlay; } /** * Update a cursor position from remote user */ updateCursor(userId: string, name: string, position: { from: number; to: number }) { const existing = this.cursors.get(userId); if (!existing) { const color = this.userColors[this.colorIndex % this.userColors.length]; this.colorIndex++; this.cursors.set(userId, { userId, name, color, position, lastUpdate: Date.now(), }); } else { existing.position = position; existing.lastUpdate = Date.now(); } // Request animation frame if not already queued if (!this.animationFrame) { this.animationFrame = requestAnimationFrame(() => this.render()); } } /** * Remove cursor when user leaves */ removeCursor(userId: string) { this.cursors.delete(userId); // Remove cursor DOM elements const element = this.overlayElement.querySelector(`[data-user-id="${userId}"]`); if (element) element.remove(); } private render() { this.animationFrame = null; // Clean stale cursors (>10 seconds old) const now = Date.now(); for (const [userId, cursor] of this.cursors) { if (now - cursor.lastUpdate > 10000) { this.removeCursor(userId); } } // Get editor's scroll and position info const editorRect = this.view.dom.getBoundingClientRect(); const scrollTop = this.view.dom.scrollTop || 0; // Render each cursor for (const [userId, cursor] of this.cursors) { const fromPos = this.view.coordsAtPos(cursor.position.from); if (!fromPos) continue; let cursorElement = this.overlayElement.querySelector( `[data-user-id="${userId}"]` ) as HTMLElement; if (!cursorElement) { cursorElement = this.createCursorElement(userId, cursor); this.overlayElement.appendChild(cursorElement); } // Position cursor const x = fromPos.left - editorRect.left; const y = fromPos.top - editorRect.top + scrollTop; cursorElement.style.transform = `translate(${x}px, ${y}px)`; // Render selection if present if (cursor.position.to > cursor.position.from) { const toPos = this.view.coordsAtPos(cursor.position.to); if (toPos) { const selectionEl = cursorElement.querySelector('.cursor-selection') as HTMLElement; if (selectionEl) { const selX = fromPos.left - editorRect.left; const selWidth = toPos.right - fromPos.left; selectionEl.style.width = `${selWidth}px`; selectionEl.style.height = `${fromPos.bottom - fromPos.top}px`; } } } } } private createCursorElement(userId: string, cursor: RemoteCursor): HTMLElement { const el = document.createElement('div'); el.dataset.userId = userId; el.style.cssText = ` position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; transition: transform 0.1s ease-out; pointer-events: none; `; // Cursor line (2px wide vertical bar) const cursorLine = document.createElement('div'); cursorLine.style.cssText = ` width: 2px; height: 1.2em; background: ${cursor.color}; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; `; // Selection highlight (if range) const selection = document.createElement('div'); selection.className = 'cursor-selection'; selection.style.cssText = ` position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; background: ${cursor.color}33; border-radius: 2px; `; // Name label const label = document.createElement('div'); label.textContent = cursor.name; label.style.cssText = ` position: absolute; top: -1.5em; left: 0; background: ${cursor.color}; color: white; padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 3px; font-size: 11px; white-space: nowrap; font-weight: 600; `; el.appendChild(cursorLine); el.appendChild(selection); el.appendChild(label); return el; } destroy() { if (this.animationFrame) { cancelAnimationFrame(this.animationFrame); } this.overlayElement.remove(); } }
sections.WebSocket Fundamentals: WebSocket provides full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection. For collaboration tools, WebSocket is superior to HTTP polling because: (1) Sub-50ms message latency vs 200-500ms for polling, (2) Server can push updates without client request, (3) Persistent connection reduces TLS handshake overhead, (4) Binary framing reduces overhead vs HTTP headers. Key considerations: (1) Socket.io provides automatic fallback to long-polling when WebSocket is blocked, (2) Use sticky sessions for horizontal scaling (or Redis adapter), (3) Heartbeat interval of 25s to detect stale connections, (4) Message ordering guarantees — Socket.io preserves order within a single transport, (5) Backpressure handling — if client is too slow, server should buffer or drop presence updates.
sections.Operational Transform (OT) Concepts: OT is the foundational technology behind Google Docs and many collaborative editors. Core principles: Operation: A unit of change (insert N chars at position P, delete M chars at position P). Operations are small, composable, and transformable. Transform Property 1 (TP1): If op1 and op2 are concurrent, then applying op1 then op2' (op2 transformed against op1) produces the same result as applying op2 then op1'. Transform Property 2 (TP2): If op1, op2, op3 are concurrent, transform(op1, transform(op2, op3)) = transform(transform(op1, op2), op3). Client-Server Model: Client sends operation to server. Server transforms against concurrent ops from other clients, applies, and broadcasts. Client receives ack with transformed version of own op. Limitations: OT becomes exponentially complex with multiple simultaneous editors. Each new concurrent op requires transformation against all others. This is why Google Docs limits to ~100 editors.
sections.CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type: CRDT provides eventual consistency without a central server. Each character is a unique node with a globally unique ID. When conflicts occur (same position), CRDT uses deterministic rules (timestamp, creator ID) to decide order — no transformation needed. Benefits over OT: (1) No central server required for correctness, (2) Naturally handles offline editing — just merge on reconnect, (3) Scales to thousands of concurrent editors, (4) Simpler implementation (no transform functions). Trade-offs: (1) Larger metadata overhead (unique IDs per character), (2) Tombstones for deleted elements consume memory, (3) Less mature ecosystem. Libraries: yjs (used by Roam Research, Obsidian), automerge (used byJupyter Notebooks).
sections.Presence Systems: Presence tracking has different performance characteristics than document sync: (1) Cursor updates are ephemeral — if lost, the next update will correct position, (2) Can tolerate higher latency (50-100ms vs 10-50ms for ops), (3) Higher frequency (20fps cursor updates vs 1-5 ops/sec typing), (4) Use separate WebSocket channel to avoid head-of-line blocking. Implementation: Presence updates sent every 50ms on a dedicated channel. Server broadcasts to room without persistence. Client renders with requestAnimationFrame for smooth animation. Fade out after 10 seconds of inactivity.
sections.Version History and Diffing: Version history is implemented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of document states. Each version stores: (1) Incremental operations since parent, (2) A full snapshot every 50 versions (checkpoint), (3) Author, timestamp, and auto-generated description. Diff between versions uses Myers' O(ND) diff algorithm. For rich text, we diff the underlying plain text and apply formatting overlays. The version tree supports branching (collaborators can diverge and merge) — displayed as a visual timeline with branch indicators.
sections.Conflict Resolution Strategies: Text (OT/CRDT): Automatic — characters never conflict because each has a unique ID. Simultaneous inserts at same position are ordered deterministically. Rich Text Formatting: Last-writer-wins for formatting attributes. If Alice bolds "hello" and Bob underlines "hello", both formats are applied. Object Fields: Last-writer-wins per field. If Alice edits title and Bob edits body, both changes are preserved. List Items: Position-based conflict resolution using fractional indexing (Figma's approach) or CRDT list. Unresolvable: When automatic resolution isn't possible (e.g., incompatible changes to same data), show merge UI similar to Git conflict markers. Users see both versions and pick the desired outcome.
sections.Scalability with WebSocket Rooms: Horizontal scaling for WebSocket connections: (1) Use Socket.io Redis adapter — broadcasts are published to Redis, each server instance subscribes and forwards to connected clients, (2) Each document is a room — operations are only sent to clients in that room, (3) For 1000+ editors per document, use hierarchical rooms: doc:123:section:A, doc:123:section:B — clients only sync the section they're viewing, (4) Load balancer with sticky sessions (WebSocket affinity), (5) Separate read-only replicas for viewers who don't need to edit.
sections.Testing Guide: // server/tests/OT.test.ts import { describe, it, expect } from '@jest/globals'; import { transform, compose } from '../src/sync/transform'; import { OperationType } from '../src/sync/operation'; describe('Operational Transform', () => { it('should transform insert before insert', () => { const op1 = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 5, text: 'AB', clientId: 'a', seq: 1 }; const op2 = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 10, text: 'CD', clientId: 'b', seq: 1 }; const [op1Prime, op2Prime] = transform(op1, op2); expect(op1Prime.position).toBe(5); expect(op2Prime.position).toBe(12); // Shifted by 2 }); it('should handle concurrent inserts at same position (tiebreak by clientId)', () => { const op1 = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 5, text: 'A', clientId: 'a', seq: 1 }; const op2 = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 5, text: 'B', clientId: 'b', seq: 1 }; const [op1Prime, op2Prime] = transform(op1, op2); // 'a' { const insert = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 3, text: 'X', clientId: 'a', seq: 1 }; const del = { type: OperationType.Delete, position: 5, length: 2, clientId: 'b', seq: 1 }; const [insPrime, delPrime] = transform(insert, del); expect(delPrime.position).toBe(6); // Shifted by insert }); it('should satisfy TP1 property', () => { const op1 = { type: OperationType.Insert, position: 3, text: 'X', clientId: 'a', seq: 1 }; const op2 = { type: OperationType.Delete, position: 5, length: 1, clientId: 'b', seq: 1 }; const [op1Prime, op2Prime] = transform(op1, op2); // Apply op1 then op2' vs op2 then op1' — same result const doc1 = applyOps('hello', [op1, op2Prime]); const doc2 = applyOps('hello', [op2, op1Prime]); expect(doc1).toBe(doc2); }); });
sections.Deployment Guide: Docker Compose: Deploy sync server (3+ instances), PostgreSQL (RDS), Redis (ElastiCache), and frontend (Vercel). Use AWS ECS with Fargate for the sync server. Load balancer with proxy protocol v2 for WebSocket support. Socket.io Redis adapter for cross-instance communication. Auto-scaling based on active connections (target: 1000 connections per instance). Monitor: (1) Operation latency P50/P95/P99, (2) Active connections per instance, (3) Redis pub/sub message rate, (4) Document state memory usage.
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Not handling concurrent operations correctly: OT transform functions must maintain TP1/TP2 properties. Many implementations handle 80% of cases but fail on edge cases like overlapping deletes or insert+delete at same position. Write property-based tests using fast-check to discover edge cases. 2. Mixing up operation ordering: The sequence of operations matters critically. Always include baseVersion in operation messages. Use vector clocks or version vectors to detect concurrent operations. Never assume operations arrive in causal order. 3. Ignoring memory usage: CRDT tombstones and operation buffers grow unboundedly. Implement snapshot compression — periodically create a full document snapshot and discard operations before it. Use Redis with maxmemory-policy for ephemeral state. 4. Blocking the main thread: CRDT merge operations on large documents (10K+ nodes) can take 10-100ms. Run heavy merge operations in a Web Worker on the client and a worker thread on the server. Use requestIdleCallback for non-critical updates. 5. No offline support in MVP: Users expect to work offline. Implement an offline queue using IndexedDB that stores pending operations. On reconnect, send all queued ops through OT transformation. Handle conflicts that arise from divergent offline edits.
sections.Interview Questions: Q1: Explain the difference between OT and CRDT for collaborative editing. A: OT transforms operations against each other so they can be applied in any order, requiring a central server to coordinate. CRDT gives each character a unique ID, so concurrent inserts naturally converge without transformation. OT is more mature (Google Docs), has less metadata overhead, but is harder to scale. CRDT is simpler for offline/peer-to-peer, handles arbitrary number of users, but uses more memory (tombstones, IDs per character). Q2: How does Google Docs handle real-time collaboration at scale? A: Google Docs uses OT with a central server. Each client sends operations to Google's servers, which transform against concurrent ops from other clients. They use a custom binary protocol over WebSocket. For scaling, they: (1) Share nothing between server instances (state in memory per document), (2) Use consistent hashing to route a document to a specific server, (3) Spawn per-document processes for isolation, (4) Limit concurrent editors to ~100 for performance. Q3: Design a system supporting 1000 simultaneous editors on one document. A: (1) Use CRDT (yjs/automerge) instead of OT for better scalability. (2) Implement virtual document sections — only sync the section each user is viewing. (3) Hierarchical WebSocket rooms: doc:123:section:5. (4) Dedicated server per document with connection pooling. (5) Operation batching — coalesce multiple operations into one message. (6) Throttle presence updates to 10fps. (7) Use Redis Streams for persistent operation log with consumer groups. Q4: How do you implement undo in a collaborative editor? A: Undo is complex because "undoing" Alice's edit shouldn't undo Bob's subsequent edit. Approach: (1) Each user has a local undo stack of their own operations. (2) When user triggers undo, generate an "inverse operation" (insert becomes delete, delete becomes insert). (3) The inverse operation is sent through OT like any other operation. (4) However, if another user's concurrent edit overlaps, the inverse might not restore the original state. (5) Better approach: selective undo — show a list of the user's recent operations and let them choose which to revert. Google Docs uses a "version history" approach instead of traditional undo. Q5: Explain how cursor synchronization works in a collaborative editor. A: Each client broadcasts their cursor position (character offset, selection start/end) at ~20fps. Position is relative to the current document state. When remote operations are applied, the cursor position must be transformed accordingly — if Alice inserts 10 chars before Bob's cursor, Bob's cursor shifts right by 10. This transformation uses the same OT transform functions. Cursors are rendered as Canvas overlays positioned using ProseMirror's coordsAtPos() — which maps document position to screen coordinates even after scrolling.
sections.Advanced Challenges: Challenge 1: Decentralized P2P Collaboration Implement peer-to-peer collaboration using WebRTC + CRDT without a central server. Use a signalling server for discovery, then direct peer-to-peer WebRTC data channels for operation sync. Handle NAT traversal with STUN/TURN. Implement mesh topology for up to 10 peers and selective forwarding for larger groups. Challenge 2: Real-Time Rich Text with Images Extend the editor to support embedded images, tables, and embeds (YouTube, Twitter). Images are uploaded to S3 during editing, and the CRDT stores the image URL with a unique content-addressed hash. Handle concurrent image uploads and deletions. Implement lazy loading for images outside the viewport. Challenge 3: Git-Like Branching & Merging Implement document branching — users can create branches for experimental edits. Show a visual branch graph similar to Git. Implement three-way merge when branches are consolidated. Allow merge requests with review workflow. This is used by Notion's "Document versions" and Coda's "Sync pages". Challenge 4: AI-Powered Collaboration Features Integrate an LLM that: (1) Suggests completions aware of other users' edits (don't suggest deleting something Bob just wrote), (2) Generates paragraph summaries for version history, (3) Detects and resolves merge conflicts by suggesting combined edits, (4) Provides inline suggestions that multiple users can review before applying. Challenge 5: Collaborative Code Editor Build a VS Code-style collaborative code editor. Challenges vs text editing: (1) AST-aware diffs — formatting changes shouldn't trigger full re-sync, (2) LSP integration — share language server results across users, (3) Terminal sharing — multiplex terminal I/O across WebSocket, (4) Dependency sync — ensure all users have the same packages installed, (5) Build output sharing — share compilation errors and test results in real-time. description: Real-time messaging app with user authentication, chat rooms, and message history using WebSockets.
title: Chat Application

sections.Project Overview: This real-time chat application project teaches you how to build a complete messaging platform from scratch using Socket.io for bidirectional real-time communication, Node.js/Express for the backend, and MongoDB for persistent message storage. Users can create accounts, join multiple chat rooms, send formatted messages with image/file attachments, see typing indicators, know when other users are online, and receive push notifications for new messages. The project covers WebSocket architecture, event-driven programming, JWT authentication, file uploads with Multer, and message pagination.
sections.Problem Statement: Real-time communication is fundamental to modern web applications - from Slack and Discord to customer support chat and collaborative tools. Building a reliable chat system requires understanding WebSocket protocols, managing concurrent connections, handling message ordering, dealing with reconnection logic, and ensuring message delivery guarantees. The challenge is building a system that handles: multiple simultaneous chat rooms, message history with infinite scroll, typing indicators that don't cause excessive events, online/offline presence detection, file/image sharing, and message delivery receipts (sent, delivered, read).
sections.Real World Use Cases: Team Collaboration: A remote team uses the chat app with channels for different projects (#frontend, #backend, #design) and direct messaging for private conversations. Customer Support: An e-commerce site integrates the chat for live customer support with agent assignment, chat queues, and pre-defined response templates. Community Platform: A gaming community creates rooms for different games, with voice chat integration, mod-only channels, and automated moderation bots.
sections.Industry Relevance: Real-time features are expected in modern web applications. Socket.io powers real-time features for millions of users at companies like Trello, Patreon, and Microsoft. Understanding WebSocket architecture is critical for building collaborative tools (Google Docs, Figma), live dashboards (Datadog, Grafana), gaming platforms, and financial trading interfaces. The event-driven patterns you learn transfer to message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka), event sourcing architectures, and real-time data pipelines.
sections.Learning Outcomes: Architect a real-time communication system using Socket.io with namespaces, rooms, and event emitters for scalable message delivery Implement JWT-based authentication with socket middleware for secure WebSocket connections and user identity verification Build a multi-room chat system with room creation, joining/leaving, and room listing with unread message counts Handle typing indicators with throttled events to prevent UI flooding and network congestion Implement online/offline presence detection with heartbeat/ping mechanism and user status broadcasting Build message formatting with markdown support (bold, italic, code blocks, mentions) and image/file attachment uploads Implement message persistence with MongoDB, including pagination with cursor-based infinite scroll and message search Handle reconnection logic with automatic rejoin of rooms, message delivery receipts, and offline message queuing
sections.Technologies Used: Socket.io Node.js + Express MongoDB + Mongoose JWT Auth React (optional) Multer + Cloudinary HTML5 CSS3
sections.Prerequisites: Intermediate JavaScript with async/await, Promises, and ES6+ modules Node.js and Express experience: routing, middleware, error handling Understanding of REST APIs and HTTP protocol basics Familiarity with MongoDB or any NoSQL database (CRUD operations, indexes) Basic understanding of authentication concepts (JWT, sessions, password hashing with bcrypt) Node.js installed (v18+) and MongoDB Atlas account (free tier)
sections.Architecture Overview: The system uses a client-server architecture with WebSocket as the primary communication channel. The Socket.io server runs alongside the Express HTTP server on the same port, sharing the authentication context. Connections are organized into namespaces (default namespace for general chat, admin namespace for moderation). Within each namespace, rooms group users by chat channel. The server maintains an in-memory map of connected users (socketId -> userId) for fast presence lookups, while persisting messages to MongoDB for history. File uploads are handled via Multer middleware and stored on Cloudinary with URLs embedded in message objects. Message delivery uses an acknowledgement pattern where the client sends an ACK after rendering, enabling read receipts. The reconnection system uses exponential backoff with jitter and rejoins all rooms on reconnection.
sections.Folder Structure: chat-app/ ├── server/ │ ├── src/ │ │ ├── index.js # Entry point, HTTP + Socket.io setup │ │ ├── socket/ │ │ │ ├── index.js # Socket.io initialization │ │ │ ├── auth.js # Socket authentication middleware │ │ │ ├── chatHandler.js # Message events │ │ │ ├── roomHandler.js # Room management │ │ │ └── presenceHandler.js # Online/offline │ │ ├── models/ │ │ │ ├── Message.js │ │ │ ├── User.js │ │ │ └── Room.js │ │ ├── routes/ │ │ │ ├── auth.js │ │ │ ├── users.js │ │ │ └── upload.js │ │ ├── middleware/ │ │ │ ├── auth.js │ │ │ └── errorHandler.js │ │ └── utils/ │ │ └── helpers.js │ ├── package.json │ └── .env ├── client/ │ ├── public/ │ ├── src/ │ │ ├── components/ │ │ ├── hooks/ │ │ ├── contexts/ │ │ └── services/ │ └── package.json ├── docker-compose.yml ├── .gitignore └── README.md
sections.Development Roadmap: Phase 1: Server Setup & Socket.io Fundamentals Initialize the Express server with Socket.io. Implement the connection/disconnection lifecycle. Create the basic event system: client emits 'chat message', server broadcasts to all clients in the room. Test with a simple HTML client that connects and sends messages. Phase 2: Authentication & User Management Implement user registration and login with JWT tokens. Add socket middleware to authenticate connections using the JWT token sent in the auth handshake. Store user profiles in MongoDB with avatars and display names. Implement guest/anon access for non-registered users. Phase 3: Room Management Implement room CRUD: create room, list rooms, join room, leave room, delete room (admin only). Messages are scoped to rooms - users only receive events for rooms they've joined. Room metadata includes name, description, topic, member count, and creation date. Phase 4: Typing Indicators & Presence Implement typing indicator events: 'typing:start' and 'typing:stop' with 2-second debounce. Broadcast only to the room. Implement online presence with socket connection/disconnection tracking. Add a heartbeat every 30 seconds to detect stale connections. Show online/away/offline status with colored indicators. Phase 5: Message History & File Uploads Store messages in MongoDB with fields: content, sender, room, timestamp, type (text/image/file), and metadata. Implement cursor-based pagination for infinite scroll. Add Multer middleware for file uploads with Cloudinary storage. Support image preview, file download, and drag-and-drop upload.
sections.Implementation Guide: Step 1: Socket.io Server with Authentication Middleware Set up the Socket.io server with JWT authentication middleware that validates tokens on connection and attaches user data to the socket object. const express = require('express'); const http = require('http'); const { Server } = require('socket.io'); const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken'); const cors = require('cors'); const app = express(); app.use(cors()); const server = http.createServer(app); const io = new Server(server, { cors: { origin: '*', methods: ['GET', 'POST'] }, pingInterval: 25000, pingTimeout: 20000 }); // Authentication middleware for Socket.io io.use((socket, next) => { const token = socket.handshake.auth.token; if (!token) { return next(new Error('Authentication required')); } try { const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET); socket.userId = decoded.userId; socket.username = decoded.username; socket.avatar = decoded.avatar; next(); } catch (err) { next(new Error('Invalid token')); } }); const onlineUsers = new Map(); // userId -> Set io.on('connection', (socket) => { console.log(`User connected: ${socket.username} (${socket.id})`); // Track online users if (!onlineUsers.has(socket.userId)) { onlineUsers.set(socket.userId, new Set()); } onlineUsers.get(socket.userId).add(socket.id); io.emit('user:online', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username }); socket.on('disconnect', () => { const userSockets = onlineUsers.get(socket.userId); if (userSockets) { userSockets.delete(socket.id); if (userSockets.size === 0) { onlineUsers.delete(socket.userId); io.emit('user:offline', { userId: socket.userId }); } } console.log(`User disconnected: ${socket.username}`); }); }); server.listen(process.env.PORT || 3000, () => { console.log('Server running on port', process.env.PORT || 3000); }); Step 2: Room Management Events Implement room creation, joining, leaving, and message broadcasting scoped to rooms. Users can be in multiple rooms simultaneously. // In-memory room store (use MongoDB for persistence) const rooms = new Map(); // Add default rooms rooms.set('general', { id: 'general', name: 'General', description: 'General discussion', createdBy: 'system', createdAt: new Date() }); rooms.set('random', { id: 'random', name: 'Random', description: 'Random topics and fun', createdBy: 'system', createdAt: new Date() }); socket.on('room:join', (roomId, callback) => { if (!rooms.has(roomId)) { return callback({ error: 'Room not found' }); } socket.join(roomId); socket.currentRooms = socket.currentRooms || []; socket.currentRooms.push(roomId); const room = rooms.get(roomId); io.to(roomId).emit('user:joined', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username, roomId }); callback({ success: true, room }); }); socket.on('room:leave', (roomId) => { socket.leave(roomId); socket.currentRooms = socket.currentRooms.filter(r => r !== roomId); io.to(roomId).emit('user:left', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username, roomId }); }); socket.on('room:create', (data, callback) => { const { name, description } = data; const id = crypto.randomBytes(8).toString('hex'); const room = { id, name, description, createdBy: socket.userId, createdAt: new Date(), memberCount: 0 }; rooms.set(id, room); socket.join(id); io.emit('room:created', room); callback({ success: true, room }); }); socket.on('room:list', (callback) => { const roomList = Array.from(rooms.values()).map(r => ({ ...r, memberCount: io.sockets.adapter.rooms.get(r.id)?.size || 0 })); callback(roomList); }); Step 3: Message Handling with Persistence and ACKs Handle message sending, storage, and delivery with read receipts. Messages are stored in MongoDB and broadcast to the room. const Message = require('../models/Message'); socket.on('message:send', async (data, callback) => { try { const { roomId, content, type = 'text', metadata = {} } = data; if (!roomId || !content?.trim()) { return callback({ error: 'Message content is required' }); } // Create and save message const message = new Message({ roomId, sender: socket.userId, senderName: socket.username, senderAvatar: socket.avatar, content: content.trim(), type, metadata, timestamp: new Date() }); await message.save(); // Broadcast to room io.to(roomId).emit('message:new', message.toObject()); // Acknowledge with message ID and timestamp callback({ success: true, messageId: message._id, timestamp: message.timestamp }); } catch (err) { callback({ error: 'Failed to save message' }); } }); // Message history with cursor-based pagination socket.on('message:history', async (data, callback) => { try { const { roomId, cursor, limit = 50 } = data; const query = { roomId }; if (cursor) { query.timestamp = { $lt: new Date(cursor) }; } const messages = await Message.find(query) .sort({ timestamp: -1 }) .limit(limit) .lean(); callback({ messages: messages.reverse(), hasMore: messages.length === limit, nextCursor: messages.length > 0 ? messages[0].timestamp : null }); } catch (err) { callback({ error: 'Failed to load messages' }); } }); // Message read receipt socket.on('message:read', async (data) => { const { messageId, roomId } = data; io.to(roomId).emit('message:read', { messageId, userId: socket.userId, readAt: new Date() }); }); Step 4: Typing Indicators with Throttling Implement typing indicators with event throttling to prevent flooding the network with typing events. Users stop typing after 2 seconds of inactivity. const typingUsers = new Map(); // roomId -> Map socket.on('typing:start', (roomId) => { if (!roomId) return; socket.to(roomId).emit('typing:update', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username, isTyping: true, roomId }); // Auto-stop typing after 3 seconds if (!typingUsers.has(roomId)) { typingUsers.set(roomId, new Map()); } const roomTyping = typingUsers.get(roomId); if (roomTyping.has(socket.userId)) { clearTimeout(roomTyping.get(socket.userId)); } roomTyping.set(socket.userId, setTimeout(() => { socket.to(roomId).emit('typing:update', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username, isTyping: false, roomId }); roomTyping.delete(socket.userId); if (roomTyping.size === 0) typingUsers.delete(roomId); }, 3000)); }); socket.on('typing:stop', (roomId) => { if (!roomId) return; const roomTyping = typingUsers.get(roomId); if (roomTyping?.has(socket.userId)) { clearTimeout(roomTyping.get(socket.userId)); roomTyping.delete(socket.userId); if (roomTyping.size === 0) typingUsers.delete(roomId); } socket.to(roomId).emit('typing:update', { userId: socket.userId, username: socket.username, isTyping: false, roomId }); }); // Client-side throttling let typingTimeout; const TYPING_THROTTLE = 1000; function handleTyping(roomId) { socket.emit('typing:start', roomId); clearTimeout(typingTimeout); typingTimeout = setTimeout(() => { socket.emit('typing:stop', roomId); }, 2000); } messageInput.addEventListener('input', () => { if (messageInput.value.trim()) { handleTyping(currentRoomId); } }); // Render typing indicators in UI socket.on('typing:update', (data) => { const indicator = document.getElementById('typing-indicator'); const typingUsers = getTypingUsers(data.roomId); if (data.isTyping) { typingUsers.set(data.userId, data.username); } else { typingUsers.delete(data.userId); } if (typingUsers.size > 0) { const names = Array.from(typingUsers.values()); indicator.textContent = names.length === 1 ? `${names[0]} is typing...` : names.length === 2 ? `${names[0]} and ${names[1]} are typing...` : `${names[0]} and ${names.length - 1} others are typing...`; indicator.style.display = 'block'; } else { indicator.style.display = 'none'; } });
sections.Testing Guide: Connection Testing: Open multiple browser tabs and connect as different users. Verify each user sees the correct online status. Disconnect a tab and verify the offline event fires. Test reconnection by stopping and restarting the server. Room Testing: Create a room from one client, verify all clients see the new room. Join multiple rooms and verify messages only appear in the correct room. Test that leaving a room stops receiving messages. Message Testing: Send messages with special characters, emoji, and long text. Verify messages persist after page refresh. Test message pagination by sending 100+ messages. Send an empty message and verify it's rejected. Test file uploads with images, PDFs, and large files. Typing Indicators: Open two tabs as different users. Type in one tab and verify the typing indicator appears in the other. Stop typing and verify the indicator disappears after 3 seconds. Test rapid typing and verify it doesn't cause multiple events. Authentication Testing: Try connecting without a token (should be rejected). Use an expired token (should be rejected). Switch users and verify the correct identity is shown. Test that disconnected users are removed from online status.
sections.Deployment Guide: Server Deployment: Deploy the Node.js server to Railway, Render, or Fly.io. These platforms support WebSocket connections natively. For traditional hosting (AWS EC2, DigitalOcean), ensure the load balancer supports WebSocket (sticky sessions or WebSocket passthrough). Set environment variables for JWT_SECRET, MONGODB_URI, and CLOUDINARY_URL. Client Deployment: If using a separate client, deploy to Vercel or Netlify. Configure the client with the server URL via environment variable. For the vanilla HTML client, update the server URL in the script. Scaling: For horizontal scaling, use Socket.io's Redis adapter to share events across multiple server instances. Set up a Redis server and configure the adapter: new RedisAdapter(io, pubClient, subClient). Use MongoDB replica sets for message persistence reliability. Monitoring: Use Socket.io's built-in metrics (io.engine.clientsCount, adapter.rooms). Set up Prometheus + Grafana for monitoring. Configure alerts for high connection counts, message latency, and error rates.
sections.Common Mistakes: 1. Not Handling Socket.io Reconnection Properly When a client reconnects after a temporary disconnect, they lose their room membership. Always listen for the 'connect' event on the client side and rejoin all previously joined rooms. Use the socket.io.reconnect() callback to restore state. Store the list of joined rooms in localStorage for recovery after page refresh. 2. Broadcasting Messages Without Server Validation Never trust client-side data. Always validate message content, room membership, and user permissions on the server before broadcasting. A malicious client could emit forged events to impersonate other users or join restricted rooms. Validate the JWT token on every reconnection and check room membership before processing message:send events. 3. Memory Leaks from Event Listener Accumulation Every time a component mounts and attaches a socket event listener, it creates a new listener. Without proper cleanup, these listeners accumulate and cause double-firing events and memory leaks. Always remove event listeners in the component cleanup function. Use socket.off() or socket.removeAllListeners() in the useEffect cleanup. 4. Excessive Typing Indicator Events Without throttling, every keystroke emits a typing event, causing 10-30 events per second per user. With 100 users typing, this floods the network. Implement client-side throttling: emit typing:start at most once per second, and auto-emit typing:stop after 2 seconds of inactivity. On the server, track typing state per user per room and debounce the stop event.
sections.Interview Questions: Q: Explain how WebSockets differ from HTTP and when you would use each. A: HTTP is a request-response protocol where the client initiates communication. Each request opens a new connection (or reuses one via keep-alive). WebSocket provides a full-duplex persistent connection over a single TCP socket, allowing the server to push data to the client without polling. Use HTTP for REST APIs, CRUD operations, and cacheable content. Use WebSocket for real-time features: chat, live notifications, collaborative editing, gaming, financial tickers, and live dashboards. WebSocket has lower latency for bidirectional communication but requires more server resources per connection. Q: How does Socket.io handle reconnection and what configuration options are available? A: Socket.io client automatically attempts reconnection when the connection drops. Key options: reconnection (boolean, default true), reconnectionAttempts (Infinity), reconnectionDelay (1000ms initial), reconnectionDelayMax (5000ms), randomizationFactor (0.5, adds jitter). The client uses exponential backoff: delay doubles with each attempt up to reconnectionDelayMax, with jitter to prevent thundering herd. On reconnection, the client re-authenticates and re-emits any buffered events. The 'reconnect' event fires on successful reconnection. Q: What is the difference between Socket.io namespaces and rooms? A: Namespaces are logical channels that create separate communication contexts on the same server. Each namespace has its own event handlers, middleware, and connection pool. Think of namespaces like separate applications (e.g., /chat, /admin, /analytics). Rooms are sub-channels within a namespace for grouping connected sockets (e.g., different chat channels). A socket can be in multiple rooms but typically joins one namespace. Rooms are created dynamically when sockets join them and are removed when empty. Use namespaces for different features, rooms for sub-grouping within a feature. Q: How do you scale Socket.io horizontally across multiple servers? A: Use Socket.io's Redis adapter to share events across server instances. The adapter uses Redis Pub/Sub: when one server emits an event, it publishes to Redis, and all other servers receive the event and forward it to their connected clients. All clients connect to the same Redis instance (or cluster). For sticky sessions, configure the load balancer to route the same client to the same server (required for long-polling transport). Configure the adapter: const { createAdapter } = require('@socket.io/redis-adapter'); const pubClient = redis.createClient(); const subClient = pubClient.duplicate(); io.adapter(createAdapter(pubClient, subClient)). Q: How would you implement end-to-end encryption in a chat application? A: Implement the Signal Protocol (used by WhatsApp and Signal) for end-to-end encrypted messaging. Each user has a public/private key pair. Messages are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a symmetric key that's exchanged using the Diffie-Hellman key exchange. Keys are stored client-side in IndexedDB. The server only sees encrypted ciphertext and cannot read messages. Implement Perfect Forward Secrecy using ephemeral keys that change with each session. Use a trusted key verification mechanism (QR code scanning or fingerprint comparison) to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks. The server handles key distribution, room membership, and delivery but never has access to plaintext.
sections.Resume Description: Architected a real-time chat platform using Socket.io with Redis adapter for horizontal scalability, supporting 10,000+ concurrent WebSocket connections across distributed server instances Implemented JWT-authenticated WebSocket connections with middleware-based token validation, user identity verification, and room-based access control for secure multi-channel communication Built an event-driven messaging system with MongoDB persistence, cursor-based pagination for infinite scroll, read receipts, and message search with text indexes Developed real-time presence detection with heartbeat-based online/offline tracking, per-room typing indicators with throttled event emission, and user status broadcasting Integrated file upload system using Multer middleware with Cloudinary storage, supporting drag-and-drop image sharing, file download with progress indicators, and automatic thumbnail generation Implemented automatic reconnection with exponential backoff and jitter, room membership restoration, and offline message queuing for reliable message delivery Designed a modular server architecture with separate handlers for chat, room management, and presence concerns, enabling independent scaling and maintenance
sections.GitHub Best Practices: README.md: Include demo GIF of the chat in action, setup instructions for server and client, environment variables documentation, WebSocket event reference table, and architecture diagram showing the data flow. Commit Messages: feat: implement JWT socket authentication middleware, fix: prevent duplicate typing indicators on reconnect, refactor: extract room handler from main socket file, perf: add Redis adapter for horizontal scaling. API Documentation: Document all Socket.io events with expected payloads and callbacks. Use a tool like Socket.io-admin-ui for debugging. Include examples of the event flow for common operations (user joins room, sends message, leaves room).
sections.Extension Ideas: Voice/Video Chat: Integrate WebRTC for peer-to-peer voice and video calls between users. Implement a signaling server using Socket.io to exchange SDP offers and ICE candidates. Add call controls (mute, speaker, hang up) and picture-in-picture mode. Message Reactions & Threads: Add emoji reactions to messages (like Slack/Discord). Implement threaded replies where clicking a message opens a thread view with all replies. Store reactions and thread metadata in MongoDB subdocuments. Chat Bots & Automation: Create a bot API that allows developers to build chat bots. Implement a weather bot, reminder bot, and poll bot as examples. Use Socket.io's bot user accounts with special permissions. Moderation System: Add message reporting, user muting/banning, profanity filtering, and rate limiting. Implement auto-moderation with keyword detection and spam filtering. Create an admin dashboard for managing users and moderating content.
